Article:
Hamas, Jihad, and Sleeping Demons: The Party at the Muslim Holy Site of Nabi Musa Sparks an Uproar
Hamas and Islamic Jihad rushed to fan the flames, and a video uploaded to social media exposed religious, generational, and cultural rifts within Palestinian society • Now, in light of the events, there is concern that the site may return to the days when it served as a hub of incitement against Jews
It happened about three weeks ago, on a Saturday night in the Judean Desert, in the heart of the wilderness, near the Nabi Musa mosque—the burial place of Moses according to ancient Muslim traditions, about ten kilometers south of Jericho. Young people from East Jerusalem on their way back to the city heard echoes of loud music from the main road. They followed the sound, reached the sacred compound, and peeked inside: dozens of men and women—some say hundreds—were dancing there in Western-style clothing, under the command of DJ Sama’ Abdulhadi, the most famous electronic music figure in the Palestinian Authority. According to one version, a fair amount of alcohol was also consumed. There was no nudity at Nabi Musa—contrary to rumors—but by traditional Palestinian standards, the dress was quite permissive. The uninvited onlookers quickly filmed the event and rushed to upload photos and videos to social media.
Within a short time, groups of young men from East Jerusalem arrived at the scene armed with wooden planks, clubs, and firecrackers. They dispersed the party with threats, insults, and shouting, claiming that the dancing desecrated the mosque. The partygoers—Christians and Muslims from Bethlehem, Ramallah, and East Jerusalem—scattered in all directions.
By the next day at noon, the presence at the site was exclusively Muslim. Hundreds of worshippers arrived at Nabi Musa and held a mass prayer there. Furniture from the khan (guesthouse) in the compound—furniture on which the revelers had sat the night before—was dragged outside and set on fire. The compound, which had been “defiled,” was “purified” with powerful water hoses. Abdulhadi herself was arrested by the Palestinian Authority on charges of desecrating religion and was later released on bail.
The 29-year-old DJ, considered a pioneer of techno and underground music in Arab society and the producer of “Boiler Room Palestine” about two years ago (which garnered six million views on YouTube), tried in vain to explain to Palestinian police that she was present at the site with a permit from the Palestinian Ministry of Tourism. The ministry, it turned out, had approved three days of filming at Nabi Musa for Sama’, a well-known local DJ, describing it as a “Palestinian heritage site.” At the same time, additional DJ sets—each four hours long—were held at other regional heritage sites, not only at Nabi Musa, by several of the Middle East’s leading DJs.
The Palestinian Prime Minister Expresses “Anger and Revulsion”
Hamas and Islamic Jihad seized on the Nabi Musa incident as a valuable prize. Palestinian Prime Minister Mohammad Shtayyeh sounded apologetic as he expressed “anger and revulsion” at what occurred at the mosque. The PA’s minister of religious affairs denied that any permit had been given to hold a “discotheque” at the mosque, while officials in Jericho explained that approval had indeed been granted to promote tourism—but that it had been exploited for other purposes.
A Reflection of Deep Undercurrents
Videos of young men and women in their twenties and thirties moving to Western rhythms in pants and tank tops brought dormant demons to the surface—religious, generational, and cultural gaps within Palestinian society. They also greatly intensified existing tensions between Christians and Muslims in PA territories, after accusations spread online that Christian youths had led the “mosque-desecrating” party. The event, it later emerged, took place within the walled domed compound but at some distance from the prayer areas and the tomb itself, closer to the khan and guest rooms. Nevertheless, for an entire week, routine Palestinian concerns were pushed off the headlines, while social media and mainstream outlets focused on what seemed a marginal event: a dance party by Palestinian youth at a religious site.
But the story did not end there. Nabi Musa—known in the previous century as an Arab nationalist focal point and a center of incitement against the British, Jews, and the Zionist movement—suddenly reverted to days peace-seekers prefer to forget. The 1920 Nabi Musa riots and other pre-state attacks against Jews in the Land of Israel often began at this site. Haj Amin al-Husseini, infamous for his later role, began his career as an inciter at Nabi Musa before being appointed mufti. The nationalist and anti-Jewish atmosphere that prevailed there was felt for many years, especially during the annual spring pilgrimage season.
In recent weeks, it seemed as though Nabi Musa had traveled decades back through a time tunnel. The compound—no longer regularly used as an active mosque in recent years and hosting tourists, partygoers, a drug rehabilitation facility, weddings, and various events—once again became a center of incitement. On the first Friday after the incident, thousands of Palestinians gathered at Nabi Musa to protest the “desecration of Islamic holy sites,” but the protest quickly devolved into incitement against Israel and Jews, with the party framed as a “Zionist conspiracy.” For the first time in many years, chants were again heard at Nabi Musa such as: “Khaybar, Khaybar, O Jews, Muhammad’s army will return,” referencing the battle in which Jews were killed by Muhammad’s followers early in Islam, and “Khaybar, Khaybar, O Jews, we shall return to Palestine.”
A week ago, in an attempt to prevent further escalation, Israel stopped buses from Hebron en route to Nabi Musa, fearing that their presence could lead to serious disturbances. Such restrictions are not unusual in the site’s history. As early as 1937, following the Arab Revolt, the British banned the traditional pilgrimage. During the Jordanian period, the Hashemite Kingdom also restricted celebrations there for many years, as did Israel, which at times prevented Jerusalem Waqf officials from holding mass events at the site.
The Arrest Backfired
Khaled Abu Toameh, journalist and senior fellow at the Jerusalem Center for Public Affairs and the Gatestone Institute, says the PA’s handling of the Nabi Musa affair amounts to a surrender to Islamist groups affiliated with Hamas and Hizb ut-Tahrir. These events, he says, reflect “deep undercurrents in the PA: Ramallah and Bethlehem, and to some extent Nablus, with more liberal populations, versus religious Hebron, and Jenin and Tulkarm, which are strongholds of Islamist conservatives.” The PA’s reaction, he adds, reveals the depth of its panic in the face of the storm Hamas whipped up—alongside accusations that the Authority itself had desecrated mosques after one of its ministries approved the event.
Abu Toameh says Abdulhadi’s arrest backfired. “It’s no surprise she was released so quickly. Within a short time, more than 100,000 people worldwide signed an online petition demanding her release, including international organizations, leading musicians, and music industry executives. The fact that the European Union previously invested five million euros in Nabi Musa only deepened the PA’s embarrassment.” The investigative committee established by the Palestinian prime minister, Abu Toameh says, “closed the matter quickly. Responsibility was placed on the Ministry of Tourism for approving the event, on the Ministry of Religious Affairs for failing to guard the site, and on the DJ for not being sensitive to Muslim feelings and for violating COVID regulations.”
Alienation from the Discourse
Author and journalist Danny Rubinstein, a Ben-Gurion University scholar and veteran researcher of Palestinian society, views the Nabi Musa affair from a slightly different angle. The heated discourse surrounding the DJ party reflects, in his view, “the decline of Palestinian nationalism, somewhat similar to the decline of Arab nationalism.” Nabi Musa—a site that historically made headlines due to national conflict—now does so because of an internal cultural clash within Palestinian society. “Many of my generation,” says Rubinstein (83), “remember Nabi Musa from Yitzhak Shami’s unforgettable novel The Revenge of the Fathers, which describes a bloody conflict between Nablus and Hebron residents over who would honor the prophet first. Suddenly, this site stands at the center of a dispute between religious conservatism and liberalism. There is deep alienation between parts of the younger generation and the old leadership—not only in national matters, but also culturally.”
Michael Milshtein, head of the Palestinian Studies Forum at the Moshe Dayan Center and author of The Green Revolution: The Social Portrait of Hamas, agrees that the Nabi Musa events are not an isolated episode but a reflection of deep transformations and tensions in the Palestinian system. They testify, he says, to “a deep gap between the older generation and the younger one.” Palestinian youth, he observes, unlike their parents, show fatigue with ideologies and slogans of struggle that once mobilized the public but failed over time to deliver significant national achievements.
“The event,” Milshtein says, “points to two parallel worlds coexisting within Palestinian society—or as many young Palestinians put it: ‘We live in one valley, and our parents and leaders live in another.’ On one side stands the older generation, clinging to traditional social and cultural values and preaching continued sacrifice for an open-ended national struggle; on the other, a younger generation with deep national awareness but largely drawn to consumer culture and alienated from political discourse. Young people in Ramallah,” Milshtein adds, “say the city has become a giant shopping mall… we no longer see pictures of martyrs or political slogans as we once did… most people can’t name four ministers or four members of the PLO or Fatah’s Central Committee.”
Against the backdrop of the Nabi Musa events, the Waqf now seeks to renew the mass pilgrimage tradition to the mosque built by Mamluk Sultan Baybars at Nabi Musa in 1268. The pilgrimage was historically scheduled—deliberately—during the Greek Orthodox Easter week, as a counterweight to Christian pilgrimages at that time.
In the past, a long procession would depart from the Temple Mount through the Lions’ Gate toward Nabi Musa on the first day of festivities, repeating this daily throughout the week. On Thursday, the procession returned to Jerusalem, and on Friday flags of the Dome of the Rock and the Prophet Muhammad were carried from Al-Aqsa and buried beneath the Rock. As noted, these religious events often ended in nationalist riots. This year, Israel and the PA are again expected to restrict the celebrations, in an effort to prevent the site’s violent history from repeating itself.
Summary: A party that took place near the Nabi Musa Mosque, the burial site of Moses according to Muslim traditions, sent shockwaves through Palestinian society and revealed gaps in religion and intergenerational age. At the party, dozens to hundreds of young Palestinian men and women danced together in Western attire while the famous DJ Sama Abd El-Hadi was responsible for the music. Young East Jerusalemites who happened to be nearby documented the event and uploaded the videos to social media. Very quickly, armed men from the Shabab arrived with sticks and clubs and dispersed the scandalous event and the revelers with threats and claims that they were desecrating the mosque. The next day, a cleansing of the site was held in the presence of Muslims only. 29-year-old Al-Hadi was arrested by the Palestinian Authority on charges of desecrating religion but was quickly released following protests that arose over her arrest. The publication of the unusual event that took place at a heritage site (apparently with the approval of the Palestinian Ministry of Tourism) drew sharp reactions from various influential figures in Palestinian society - from Palestinian Prime Minister Mohammad Shtayyeh to Hamas and Islamic Jihad movements, who were shocked by the young people dancing at the heritage site. According to Khaled Abu Toameh, the entire commotion surrounding the party revealed deep gaps in Palestinian society, between more liberal elements, young people living in Ramallah and Bethlehem, and more conservative and religious elements in Tulkarem and Hebron. Another issue that occurred was the re-establishment of the religious site as a center for incitement against Israel and Jews. The site, which is traditionally a pilgrimage destination, had already served during the British Mandate as a source for inciting the people against the government. Michael Milstein also believes that the event accurately reflects the changes and deep tensions within the Palestinian system. According to him, the younger generation is tired of ideologies and slogans of struggle that previously motivated the parent generation. The youth, although possessing a deep national awareness, are also drawn to a more material and liberal world.
Sentence in Summary: 29-year-old Al-Hadi was arrested by the Palestinian Authority on charges of desecrating religion but was quickly released following protests that arose over her arrest
Best Match Sentences From Article: Abdulhadi herself was arrested by the Palestinian Authority on charges of desecrating religion and was later released on bail

Article:
My Big Family

The family car has accompanied us for decades, as have leisure vehicles, which have gained popularity in recent years • What are the important things to remember when choosing a car for the family? • And how do you check if the car you are interested in is suitable for you? • Don't get into a car without this guide

Until recently, it was simple. During the 1980s, when the people of Israel began to buy cars in droves, the basic and preferred standard was established here: family car (category C), sedan structure (four doors and a separate trunk), 1.6-liter engine, and automatic transmission. Family cars have always boasted the space they offer in the back seat and trunk and comfort during long trips.

Those engines have been replaced over the years. Almost all car manufacturers now offer turbocharged engines, which promise more power alongside savings. At the same time, the hybrid movement has grown in recent years, allowing for significant fuel savings, thanks to charging the engine with electricity.

One of the prominent issues in every family's considerations is the safety rating of the car. This parameter has also undergone significant upgrades in recent years, and alongside the requirement to have a score of 5 stars in the Euro NCAP test, every car manufacturer provides updated safety equipment, which is both alerting and active - helping to neutralize one of the main causes of accidents: loss of attention. You just need to pay attention to the level of equipment included in the car you chose according to the trim level.

Leisure Vehicle: Yes or No?

The family car that has accompanied us is gradually being replaced by the leisure vehicle. Its characteristics are the ability to combine the features of the family car with the adventurous look characteristic of an off-road vehicle. Since the compact leisure vehicle has been offered at the price of a family car (Kia Sportage 2015, for example), the market has reacted with a significant fluctuation in price levels, and the segment has risen: leisure vehicles are encroaching on family car sales in the sales tables. Just before closing the issue, following the growing preference for leisure vehicles over family cars, family car importers began to lower prices by 5-10 percent. In other cases, they raised the level of equipment offered to customers.

Compared to the family car it is based on, the compact leisure vehicle is less comfortable to drive, less successful dynamically, and its price is 5-10 percent higher than that of family cars. Adding to this is the fact that its fuel consumption is generally higher, leaving us with many question marks regarding the advisability of purchasing these vehicles.

On the other hand, the leisure vehicle has better interior space and offers an improved driving position, comfortable entry and exit, and of course the option to travel on trails, although sometimes it is advisable to take the manufacturers' statements on this matter with a grain of salt. It's not a Jeep.

Family Cars Yes

Family car or leisure vehicle? As in every article in this supplement, we could write a scroll about the huge and confusing offer, but let's get straight to the practicalities.

The challenge begins already in the showroom. Arrive there at least in pairs, hold a notebook in hand, write down your opinions, and compare them only at the end of the review. Upon entering the tested car, feel the driver's environment, the driving position, the options available to adjust the seat, and the headroom from the ceiling - especially in cars with a sunroof. Also pay attention to the right knee space in relation to the center console, and the ease of entering and exiting the car.

It is also important to check the human engineering components, pay attention to the quality of materials and assembly, the available space in the storage compartments, and of course - how spacious the back seat is. It is also important to check the trunk volume, especially if you are at a stage where you need to hold two baby strollers at the same time.

Do not skip going on as many test drives as possible, preferably on the same route, which should go through rough terrain or bumpy roads and urban and intercity roads, as many models do not manage well with the quality of paving and maintenance of city streets and roads in Israel. During the drive, turn off the radio and mobile phone, and listen to the car and the outside noises that penetrate it. Pay attention to its performance on each of the routes. Check how much the car struggles during overtaking or prolonged climbs, and how much you and the passengers feel the bumps in the asphalt.

It is also important to feel the steering. A steering wheel that does not convey any feeling undermines confidence, especially outside the city. A steering wheel that has a dead zone (one that does not turn the wheels) that is too large does not contribute to a sense of calm.

You Pay for Recklessness
Financing plans are flooding us from every direction, and promises of buying a new car with a monthly expense that seems easy to load onto our bank account are making the purchase of a new car more tempting than ever. The financing plans offered today usually come with reasonable terms and cover the full vehicle price, so there is no obligation to arrive with a down payment. Your task is to remain skeptical, read the fine print, and above all — compare. A survey of financing offers from different companies is highly recommended.
It is also worth checking “zero-kilometer” deals from leasing companies, whose advantage lies in a lower price due to an additional ownership registration. On the other hand, these vehicles are usually offered with a lower trim level, both in safety and comfort.
Bring the warranty component to the negotiating table as well, and make sure it is at least three years (the default), with no mileage limitation. In a hybrid vehicle, the battery warranty should be at least five years.
It is also advisable to prepare for depreciation — the Achilles’ heel of buying a new car — and to check how well the vehicle retains its value after three years. In this context, some importers offer trade-in deals at list price that are certainly worth examining. Also check how your car ranks on the liquidity index — how quickly it can be sold in the future — which to begin with is not particularly high.
We present several proposals for family cars and compact leisure vehicles. All have advantages, disadvantages, and a bottom line for your judgment. These can teach not only about the specific model discussed, but also help focus attention on the key components in choosing a car. The prices listed are the recommended consumer prices for the base trim level. Often, you can reduce them further through a cash discount, visits to dealerships during special sales days, or assistance from large consumer organizations. And above all — drive safely.
• • •
Opel Astra
Pros: Efficient vehicle with good safety and comfort equipment and a high trim level; good dynamic ability and respectable performance. Cons: No advanced safety equipment in the base trim; ride comfort is average; road and wind noise are too prominent. Bottom line: A well-equipped and safe family car — among the best in its class. Price: from ₪132,000
Toyota Corolla
Pros: Ride comfort is generally reasonable, performance is adequate. Safety equipment has been updated, with generous comfort features added. Cons: Engine noise is noticeable under load; ride comfort is less impressive in more demanding conditions. No rear air vents. Bottom line: A family car that offers good value for money, and is now safer than before. Price: from ₪126,000
Hyundai Ioniq
Pros: New hybrid model with excellent fuel consumption, outstanding warranty, safe handling, and good equipment. Cons: Marginal headroom for tall passengers; trunk is inconvenient to use; stiff in city driving; road noise is clearly audible outside the city; average interior design. Bottom line: A safe car that promises long-term savings. Price: from ₪136,000
Mazda 3
Pros: Rich and advanced safety and comfort equipment; good dynamic ability; updated exterior and interior design; strong performance with the 2.0-liter engine; good ride comfort. Cons: Rear space is somewhat tight compared to other family cars; trunk is on the smaller side. The 1.5-liter engine lacks punch; road and wind noise are noticeable outside the city. Bottom line: A well-equipped, capable, enjoyable, and safe family car. Price: from ₪132,000
Skoda Octavia

Article:
My Big Family

The family car has accompanied us for decades, as have leisure vehicles, which have gained popularity in recent years • What are the important things to remember when choosing a car for the family? • And how do you check if the car you are interested in is suitable for you? • Don't get into a car without this guide

Until recently, it was simple. During the 1980s, when the people of Israel began to buy cars in droves, the basic and preferred standard was established here: family car (category C), sedan structure (four doors and a separate trunk), 1.6-liter engine, and automatic transmission. Family cars have always boasted the space they offer in the back seat and trunk and comfort during long trips.

Those engines have been replaced over the years. Almost all car manufacturers now offer turbocharged engines, which promise more power alongside savings. At the same time, the hybrid movement has grown in recent years, allowing for significant fuel savings, thanks to charging the engine with electricity.

One of the prominent issues in every family's considerations is the safety rating of the car. This parameter has also undergone significant upgrades in recent years, and alongside the requirement to have a score of 5 stars in the Euro NCAP test, every car manufacturer provides updated safety equipment, which is both alerting and active - helping to neutralize one of the main causes of accidents: loss of attention. You just need to pay attention to the level of equipment included in the car you chose according to the trim level.

Leisure Vehicle: Yes or No?

The family car that has accompanied us is gradually being replaced by the leisure vehicle. Its characteristics are the ability to combine the features of the family car with the adventurous look characteristic of an off-road vehicle. Since the compact leisure vehicle has been offered at the price of a family car (Kia Sportage 2015, for example), the market has reacted with a significant fluctuation in price levels, and the segment has risen: leisure vehicles are encroaching on family car sales in the sales tables. Just before closing the issue, following the growing preference for leisure vehicles over family cars, family car importers began to lower prices by 5-10 percent. In other cases, they raised the level of equipment offered to customers.

Compared to the family car it is based on, the compact leisure vehicle is less comfortable to drive, less successful dynamically, and its price is 5-10 percent higher than that of family cars. Adding to this is the fact that its fuel consumption is generally higher, leaving us with many question marks regarding the advisability of purchasing these vehicles.

On the other hand, the leisure vehicle has better interior space and offers an improved driving position, comfortable entry and exit, and of course the option to travel on trails, although sometimes it is advisable to take the manufacturers' statements on this matter with a grain of salt. It's not a Jeep.

Family Cars Yes

Family car or leisure vehicle? As in every article in this supplement, we could write a scroll about the huge and confusing offer, but let's get straight to the practicalities.

The challenge begins already in the showroom. Arrive there at least in pairs, hold a notebook in hand, write down your opinions, and compare them only at the end of the review. Upon entering the tested car, feel the driver's environment, the driving position, the options available to adjust the seat, and the headroom from the ceiling - especially in cars with a sunroof. Also pay attention to the right knee space in relation to the center console, and the ease of entering and exiting the car.

It is also important to check the human engineering components, pay attention to the quality of materials and assembly, the available space in the storage compartments, and of course - how spacious the back seat is. It is also important to check the trunk volume, especially if you are at a stage where you need to hold two baby strollers at the same time.

Do not skip going on as many test drives as possible, preferably on the same route, which should go through rough terrain or bumpy roads and urban and intercity roads, as many models do not manage well with the quality of paving and maintenance of city streets and roads in Israel. During the drive, turn off the radio and mobile phone, and listen to the car and the outside noises that penetrate it. Pay attention to its performance on each of the routes. Check how much the car struggles during overtaking or prolonged climbs, and how much you and the passengers feel the bumps in the asphalt.

It is also important to feel the steering. A steering wheel that does not convey any feeling undermines confidence, especially outside the city. A steering wheel that has a dead zone (one that does not turn the wheels) that is too large does not contribute to a sense of calm.

You Pay for Recklessness
Financing plans are flooding us from every direction, and promises of buying a new car with a monthly expense that seems easy to load onto our bank account are making the purchase of a new car more tempting than ever. The financing plans offered today usually come with reasonable terms and cover the full vehicle price, so there is no obligation to arrive with a down payment. Your task is to remain skeptical, read the fine print, and above all — compare. A survey of financing offers from different companies is highly recommended.
It is also worth checking “zero-kilometer” deals from leasing companies, whose advantage lies in a lower price due to an additional ownership registration. On the other hand, these vehicles are usually offered with a lower trim level, both in safety and comfort.
Bring the warranty component to the negotiating table as well, and make sure it is at least three years (the default), with no mileage limitation. In a hybrid vehicle, the battery warranty should be at least five years.
It is also advisable to prepare for depreciation — the Achilles’ heel of buying a new car — and to check how well the vehicle retains its value after three years. In this context, some importers offer trade-in deals at list price that are certainly worth examining. Also check how your car ranks on the liquidity index — how quickly it can be sold in the future — which to begin with is not particularly high.
We present several proposals for family cars and compact leisure vehicles. All have advantages, disadvantages, and a bottom line for your judgment. These can teach not only about the specific model discussed, but also help focus attention on the key components in choosing a car. The prices listed are the recommended consumer prices for the base trim level. Often, you can reduce them further through a cash discount, visits to dealerships during special sales days, or assistance from large consumer organizations. And above all — drive safely.

Pros: Efficient vehicle with good safety and comfort equipment and a high trim level; good dynamic ability and respectable performance. Cons: No advanced safety equipment in the base trim; ride comfort is average; road and wind noise are too prominent. Bottom line: A well-equipped and safe family car — among the best in its class. Price: from ₪132,000
Toyota Corolla
Pros: Ride comfort is generally reasonable, performance is adequate. Safety equipment has been updated, with generous comfort features added. Cons: Engine noise is noticeable under load; ride comfort is less impressive in more demanding conditions. Article:
How much will a ski vacation in Europe cost you? Seven prominent ski resorts - what they offer, who they are suitable for, and how much it costs? Who among us, living in the Middle East, does not dream of waking up one winter morning to the sound of absolute silence outside, opening the window, and seeing everything covered in a soft white blanket? In the meantime, this is reserved for the few who live in the Jerusalem area or the Golan Heights and enjoy a white visit almost every winter, but for everyone else, especially those looking to enjoy the snow with other activities like skiing, the best option is the ski resorts in Europe. Want to receive more updates? Join Israel Hayom on Facebook. Many Israelis have already become addicted to the annual ski vacation, and if you also feel like trying, now is the perfect time to book your next vacation. To make your choice easier, we have surveyed several ski resorts in Europe and the various packages you can purchase in the country. Almost every resort has several accommodation options, including “all-inclusive” accommodation, which usually includes flights, transfers to the resort, lodging, meals, and a ski pass that allows access to the ski area. Another option is to assemble ski packages independently, which may reduce the overall cost. In any case, wherever you decide to ski, do yourself a favor and stop for coffee or wine at one of the restaurants scattered across the mountain. It’s a true indulgence on a sunny, pleasant day and will provide memories just as enjoyable as the skiing itself. France: Les Arcs: Les Arcs is a ski resort in the French Alps offering accommodation at various elevations — 1,600 meters, 1,800 meters, or 2,000 meters. The resort is highly developed and features a wide range of ski runs, from easy beginner slopes to black runs for professionals. In total, there are 112 runs covering no less than 425 kilometers of skiing. If you’re experiencing skiing for the first time, the resort offers ski schools and even private lessons for all ages. After skiing, you can enjoy restaurants and inns spread throughout the resort, small shops, a local bakery, and bars. Price: from €1,079 via “Ski Deal” What’s included? Flight, transfers to the resort, hotel (including breakfast and dinner), ski pass, ski equipment transport, and a discount on on-site equipment rental. Cheapest package: For €899, you can book a package including flight, transfers, 7 nights in a studio apartment for four people, a 6-day ski pass, and ski equipment transport. Bulgaria: Bansko. The town of Bansko is located about 160 km from Bulgaria’s capital, Sofia. The resort offers many runs (65 km) suitable for beginners and intermediate skiers, but only one black run for advanced skiers. Snowboarders will find a dedicated snow park, as well as ski-walking trails.
After skiing, you can visit a 500-year-old church, enjoy modern cafés, gourmet restaurants, and vibrant nightlife. Price: €699–€839, depending on hotel rating, via “Easy Go Tourism & Direct Vacations” What’s included? Flight, transfers, 7 nights at a hotel, 6-day ski pass, 6 ski lessons (4 hours per day), and equipment for 6 days. Andorra: Grandvalira. The Grandvalira ski area is the largest ski destination in Andorra, offering standards comparable to well-known Alpine resorts — both in size (210 km of runs) and infrastructure, with an efficient and advanced lift system. Most hotels are located near the lifts and slopes, which cater to skiers of all levels. Beyond skiing, visitors can enjoy architecture, nature, gastronomy, culture, and entertainment. Andorra is duty-free, and its capital, Andorra la Vella, offers a wide variety of shopping options. Price: €949–€1,029 via Issta, depending on town and hotel rating What’s included? Article:
Now That Prices Have Dropped, Which Streamer Should You Choose? Stream of consciousness: With all due respect to smart TVs, anyone who wants to stream movies from their computer to the living-room screen still needs a streamer.
Over the past two years, many customers have disconnected from satellite and cable providers. Instead of paying hundreds of shekels, they upgrade their internet speed and search for content on their own online. The question is: how do you transfer the movies and TV series you downloaded to the computer in your home office to the television in the living room?
Want more updates? Join Israel Hayom on Facebook.
The first—and least aesthetic—option is to connect the computer directly to the TV. The simpler and more successful option is a streamer: a small device whose purpose is to transfer content from a personal computer to the TV screen and the living-room sound system.
It’s true that some smart TVs now have a built-in streamer, but the downside is that you have to copy every movie to an external hard drive and connect it to the TV. Another drawback is that some of these TVs do not support rich audio formats embedded in the file itself (DTS). In the best case, the TV will convert it to stereo. In the worst case, you won’t hear anything at all.
Streamers promise conversion and support for almost every existing audio and video format, and they also provide firmware updates to support new formats that enter the market.
Streamer prices have dropped significantly over the past two years, ranging from about 600 shekels for basic models up to 2,000 shekels. There are even cheaper devices—mostly Android-based products from China—but they’re less user-friendly and not all of them support Hebrew.

Here, There, and Everywhere
Every respectable streamer includes a variety of video and audio outputs, several USB ports, an HDMI output, and in some cases even a memory card slot. After physically connecting the streamer to the TV and home theater system, we connect it to the home Wi-Fi network.
You define for the streamer the locations of the video, music, and photo folders on your computer—and the content starts flowing. Download a TV series you want to watch (in an MKV file), and the streamer will already be playing it on the TV before you’ve even had time to get up from your chair and move to the couch.
Unlike in the not-so-distant past, streaming HD-quality movies over a wireless network works perfectly on all the models we reviewed. You can also connect a hard drive directly to the streamer, and on some models you can even install an internal hard drive.
Over the years, streamers have evolved and are now able to consume content from the internet as well. True, not all websites are accessible, browsing the web with a remote control is difficult, and that’s what Chromecast or Apple TV are really meant for—but the option exists if you really want it.
Some streamers are equipped with an Android operating system in addition to the main interface, but this isn’t a particularly successful feature. In our experience, there aren’t many Android apps you can install, and those that do work tend to freeze from time to time.
The major differences between streamers lie in the hardware specifications, but the interface and user experience can make all the difference. One offers an Android system, another boasts a casing that prevents overheating, and a third scans all the movies on your computer and builds a virtual library with images and descriptions for each film—just like we always dreamed.
Convinced you need a streamer? Now the question is which one is best. We went out to check what’s available on the market and which models are most recommended.

1st Place: Xtreamer Sidewinder 3
What started as a cheap alternative to Popcorn has improved significantly in recent versions. Simple design in a small device made of materials that prevent overheating. Slow internet browser, fast radio, and a music player that mainly supports MP3 files and struggles with other formats. Software updates are annoying and can only be done via USB—not over the internet. Android support is limited to the outdated version 2.2, which isn’t worth much.
Pros: Supports SDHC memory cards, USB 3.0, Wi-Fi dongle included in the price Cons: Annoying firmware updates, clumsy browsing of shared folders from the computer Price: 699 shekels (Bug stores, one-year warranty) Bottom line: The best value of the bunch, despite somewhat cumbersome content browsing.

2nd Place: Popcorn C-200
The “Intel” of streamers—a veteran company that continues to improve. The C-200 is a slightly outdated model, but there isn’t a format it doesn’t recognize. Installing software called myiHome on the computer scans your movie collection and builds an impressive virtual library with movie images and category sorting.
A relatively large device, but it includes a bay for installing a hard drive, DVD drive, or Blu-ray drive in place of the old DVD player. Modern design, LCD panel, and an RF remote control (doesn’t require line-of-sight).
Pros: The most user-friendly interface Cons: Physically large device; does not include a Wi-Fi dongle (sold separately for 75 shekels) Price: 999 shekels (KSP stores, one-year warranty) Bottom line: Classic and user-friendly. Those willing to invest time and money will reap the benefits, despite it being slightly outdated and bulky.

3rd Place: WD TV Live
Western Digital was one of the first companies to identify the market’s potential and only recently added Hebrew support. After disappointment with earlier models, the new version is a pleasant surprise. Tiny, fast, with built-in Wi-Fi and a refreshed, convenient interface.
Setting up shared folders from the computer is only possible after installing software called WD Discovery, but once you understand how it works, streaming begins smoothly.
Pros: Small and clever—just like its remote Cons: Somewhat clumsy computer folder connection; slow interface Price: 599 shekels (Benda Magnetic, two-year warranty) Bottom line: Small and clever. A price cut of 100 shekels would have pushed it to first place. A positive surprise, though the interface is a bit slow.

4th Place: Apple TV
Less of a streamer and more of a platform for purchasing content or TV channels—such as NBA, MLB, or Netflix (not yet supported in Israel)—which Apple provides for a fee. A typically “Apple” product: efficient and flawless, but unfortunately media streaming from a computer must go through iTunes, which works great for photos and music but is problematic for video.
Without jailbreaking, the device does not support MKV files, only formats like MOV. Those willing to pay for content will enjoy it: HD movie playback is smooth and includes Hebrew subtitle support.
AirPlay allows mirroring the iPhone or iPad screen to the TV in excellent quality and without interruptions.
Pros: Excellent internet streaming Cons: Less successful as a traditional streamer; no option to connect an external hard drive Price: 559 shekels (iDigital, one-year warranty) Bottom line: Elegant and smooth. For Apple fans
Summary: The article discusses the increasing use of streamers as a substitute for a direct connection of a computer to a television or as a replacement for smart TVs for viewing content from the computer. The article notes that streamers are preferable to smart TVs because they support more audio and video formats, provide hardware updates, and eliminate the need to copy movies to an external hard drive. The prices of streamers have dropped in recent years and range from 600 to 2,000 shekels, and there are also cheaper devices that are less convenient to use. Streamers connect to the television and the home network, allowing streaming of video, music, and images from the computer to the television. Some streamers also allow content consumption from the internet, but not all websites support this, and browsing the internet using the remote is not convenient. The article mentions that the Android operating system on streamers is not particularly successful. The differences between the various streamers focus on hardware, user interface, and additional features. The article reviews four models of streamers: Xtreamer Sidewinder 3, Popcorn C-200, WD TV Live, and Apple TV. The Xtreamer Sidewinder 3 is a simple and cheap streamer that supports SDHC memory cards and USB3, but browsing its content folders is somewhat cumbersome. The Popcorn C-200 is a high-quality and user-friendly streamer with a convenient interface, but it is physically large and does not include a WiFi dongle. The WD TV Live is a small and agile streamer with built-in WiFi, but the connection to folders on the computer is somewhat cumbersome and its interface is a bit slow. The Apple TV is an elegant and sleek product that is mainly suitable for Apple fans, but it is less successful as a streamer and does not allow connection of an external drive.
Sentence in Summary: The article reviews four models of streamers: Xtreamer Sidewinder 3, Popcorn C-200, WD TV Live, and Apple TV

Article: “You gained weight": The host responded to a rude follower. Journalist Noa Rosin shared with her Instagram followers a message she received from a user who informed her that she had gained weight, and received a huge and supportive hug from her friends in the industry and from followers. • "I will not give up my joy of life and my love for food" "Dear Noa, I'm sorry to inform you, the weight gain": this is how a user chose to "compliment" the food journalist and morning show host Noa Rosin, in a private message he sent her on Instagram, not forgetting to close the lovely message with a rude emoji. Instead of being offended by the ugly keyboard bullying of that user, Rosin chose to share the post and used it - not only to talk about herself but to convey a positive message about body acceptance and positive self-image. • Eurovision 2019: All updates • Erez Tal is amazed: "This is a monster I didn't know" • Breaking a record: phenomenal exposure to Eurovision "One of the messages I receive constantly is: 'How do you look like this if you eat all the time?" Rosin opened the post. "At first, I always joke, saying that it’s all about the angles of the photo, and then I tell the truth: two strength training sessions a week, and recently also two Pilates classes. Then comes a response like this, which, despite all the compliments I receive, manages to ruin the joy. The joy of eating, the joy of life. Article: Did Alyssa Milano fake having COVID-19? The actress claimed in an Instagram post that she received three negative test results for the virus, until antibodies were finally found in her blood confirming that she had been sick • "She's faking it," claimed a user. Actress Alyssa Milano posted a long and charged post on her Instagram last Thursday, in which she claimed that the COVID-19 testing system in the U.S. is flawed and that the number of patients is much higher than what is published. # instagram = Yes [instagram] View this post on Instagram A post shared by Alyssa Milano (@milano_alyssa) on Aug 5, 2020 at 12:42pm PDT In the photo she posted, Milano (47) is seen lying in bed with an oxygen mask on her face. The text accompanying the photo was a long and pointed manifesto about what she described as the government's faulty testing system, providing her personal story as an example. "This is what I looked like on April 2 after being sick for two weeks," Milano opened the post. "I have never been this sick. Everything hurt, I had no sense of smell. I felt like an elephant was sitting on my chest, I couldn't breathe or digest food. I lost 4 kg in two weeks," Milano describes the severe symptoms of the virus. • "It's still cancer": Alyssa Milano lashes out at Matt Damon • Alyssa Milano is not happy with the new Charmed • The horrors of Harvey Weinstein are revealed "I was confused, I had a low fever and the headaches were terrible. I had every possible symptom of the virus. At the end of March, I took two tests for the virus and both came back negative. I also took an antibody test and it came back negative. For four months, I suffered from persistent symptoms of the virus. Vertigo, stomach pain and digestive issues, irregular periods, heart palpitations, shortness of breath, almost complete loss of short-term memory, and a general terrible feeling of weakness and depression," Milano continued detailing the illness she experienced, sharing that she insisted on another type of antibody test. This time it confirmed what she had known all along: she was positive for the virus. "I am positive for the virus antibodies," she wrote in capital letters. "I have COVID-19." From this point in the post, Milano shifted from describing her personal case to an attack on the healthcare system and the testing method, which she called flawed and deceptive. "I want you to be aware that our testing method is flawed and we do not know the real numbers," she wrote. "I also want you to know that this disease is not a hoax. I was sure I was dying, I really felt like I was going to die," she detailed. "I will donate my plasma in hopes that I might save lives. Please take care of yourselves. Please wash your hands and make sure to wear a mask and practice social distancing. I don't want anyone to feel like I felt. Feel better, love you all (maybe except for the trolls, just the kind people)." She ended the post. As expected, the revealing post quickly went viral, garnering over 197,000 likes and hundreds of comments including from her colleagues: "Oh babe, I'm sorry you were so sick," wrote actress Reese Witherspoon, "but thank you for helping us be informed. We need to know more." "Thank you for sharing this with us and I'm glad you're feeling better," wrote actress Jamie-Lynn Sigler, and Chelsea Handler added: "Glad to hear you're okay." Alongside hundreds of supportive comments, there were also those who claimed that Milano, known not only as an actress but also as a particularly vocal political and social activist who is not afraid to speak out against the government and its leader, was lying about the illness or at least exaggerating in her descriptions, and did so mainly to generate headlines. "She's lying," tweeted a user named Carlos Osweda, claiming that on the dates when Milano claims she was lying in bed almost on her deathbed, she had no problem being interviewed and promoting her business activities and broadcasting a health status far from that of a dying woman. # tweet = Yes [tweet] Many users supported him and quickly claimed that Milano was obsessively seeking attention. "She just wants attention," wrote one user, "people need to stop giving it to her. Stop feeding the troll." "After 36 years of listening to people who can't breathe," wrote another commenter who is likely involved in the medical field and who listened to the podcast in which the star participated, "she sounds to me like she is breathing without any difficulty. In fact, people who experience breathing difficulties can't really talk, and rarely sound that good." Another user also commented on Milano's voice: "I work in respiratory therapy and this is not an oxygen mask. This is an inhalation mask for respiratory treatments. So after hearing her voice on the podcast, she could breathe just fine and was not in any respiratory distress. She's a liar." There were also quite a few users who came out against Osweda's "blame game," including actress Patricia Arquette, who wished him "to get what he deserves." Milano herself did not remain indifferent to the aggressive tweet against her and tweeted in response: "Are you kidding? Why would I make up that I had the virus?" Today (Sunday), Milano posted another tweet of her lying in bed in the emergency room with a mask, updating her followers that she is still suffering from the virus symptoms, and since she felt pressure in her chest over the weekend, she decided to go to the emergency room to check that it was not a blood clot. • Summer hits 2020: Disconnected, we are tired of you • The actress who wanted to leave "Friends" Milano is mainly known for her roles in the series "Who's the Boss" (which made headlines last week when we announced she would return for a sequel three decades after it first aired), "Melrose Place" and "Charmed." In addition to her on-screen appearances, she is also a vocal and energetic political and social activist, she was the one who created the viral hashtag for the famous "Me Too" campaign and even graced the cover of "Time" magazine in 2017, in an issue focused on "Silence Breakers" (alongside women like Taylor Swift, Ashley Judd, and Selma Blair).
Summary: Alyssa Milano shared in an Instagram post that she was seriously ill with COVID-19 but received negative test results, until an antibody test confirmed that she had indeed contracted the virus. Milano described the severe symptoms she suffered from for four months, including difficulty breathing, memory loss, dizziness, digestive issues, and more. The post went viral and sparked many reactions, including supportive responses from colleagues in the industry as well as skeptical comments claiming that Milano, known as a political activist, fabricated or exaggerated her illness. It was claimed that Milano sounded and acted normally during the time she alleged she was seriously ill, and that she might have been using an inhalation mask rather than an oxygen mask as she claimed. Milano responded to the allegations against her and stated that there was no reason for her to fabricate her illness, and even posted another tweet updating that she went to the emergency room due to concerns about a blood clot. Beyond her acting career, Milano is also a social and political activist, and she is the one who initiated the MeToo campaign.
Sentence in Summary: Milano responded to the allegations against her and stated that there was no reason for her to fabricate her illness, and even posted another tweet updating that she went to the emergency room due to concerns about a blood clot

Why would I make up that I had the virus?" Article:
Applause? Wait a moment: A summary of Miri Regev's term

She broke an Olympic record (of embarrassment), called artists to order (without reading a word), brought crowds to the cinema (to see a film she opposed), and encouraged the music world (through forbidden PR) • 15 moments we will never forget from Regev's term as Minister of Culture and Sport

Love her or hate her, there has never been a minister who attracted more media attention than outgoing Culture Minister Miri Regev. Despite the criticism of her term, no one can say it was boring.

To summarize the most turbulent period the Ministry of Culture and Sport has ever known, we collected the surprising moments, the embarrassing moments, and the most embarrassing moments of the outgoing minister, and although we covered her non-stop, we were also surprised by how many there were and had to settle for the top 15.

June 2015

Let the Games Begin

Regev began her term with a nationalistic cultural gesture representing M. Cyrus, when in one of her first interviews in the role she already showed everyone she was coming in like a wrecking ball, when she was quoted saying: "In the world of culture, people are uptight, hypocritical, scheming, ungrateful. I didn't come to work for ingrates, but that's probably the lesson the Holy One Blessed Be He set for me".

September 2015

Stories of Chekhov, like a stone that has no turning

A wise man once said, "Nothing unites as strongly as hatred; not love, not friendship, nor admiration." Miri Regev understood well the power of this unifying tool and in an interview with "Israel Hayom" she declared a cultural revolution she was planning. "I will come to certain institutions and kind of tell them, how many times a week did you eat steak? From now on, eat half of that, and the rest will go to a few other institutions that until now only ate chicken, and they also want to enjoy steak." This statement was perceived as a declaration of war against the "cultural elite," and indeed she did not delay in coming with full force in the following months. By the way, the wise man who said the statement at the beginning of this section is the famous playwright Anton Chekhov, the same one that in that interview Miri declared she had never read.

"I am Miri Siboni Regev from Kiryat Gat, I have never read Chekhov, I hardly went to plays in my childhood, I listened to Joe Amar and Spanish songs, and I am no less cultured than any consumer of Western culture" // Photo: Maya Baumel Birger

July 2016

Control Games

After a slow and torturous demise, for her and for all of us, the Israel Broadcasting Authority returned its soul to the broadcasting schedule and in its place, the Kan broadcasting corporation was established. But even before it was established, politicians worked for it on a special prescription of Postinor. David Bitan determined that "we checked the Facebook of the corporation's senior officials and they are leftists," Communications Minister Ayoub Kara urged that "we need to think twice about why this is necessary," but trust Minister Regev to issue the statement that redefined press freedom: "What is the value of the broadcasting corporation if we do not control it?".

Democracy begins with control // Photo: Gideon Markovich

August 2016

Eyal Golan is Calling You

Minister Regev has always been a pioneer in her field. Long before Naama Boker struggled with mosquitoes on "Survivor" and Oren Hazan made guest appearances on "Big Brother," it was Miri Regev who dipped her fingers into the reality show genre, when a video featuring her was published in which she called on viewers to watch the new season of the show "Eyal Golan is Calling You." This spontaneous promo surprised the Knesset Ethics Committee, which determined that the minister "crossed the line into forbidden PR," thus violating the prohibition that Knesset members are not allowed to promote commercial content. Later, Regev made sure to pad Golan's pocket with several tens of thousands of shekels for performing two songs at the "Crown of the East" festival, and the PR scandal was forgotten.

The Ministry of Culture and Sport and Public Relations // Screenshot

August 2016

Olympic Record

In 2004, when windsurfer Gal Friedman ascended the podium to receive Israel's first Olympic gold medal, Education, Culture and Sport Minister Limor Livnat rushed towards him in a display of bad taste that broke records in the eyes of many. But in sports, records are meant to be broken, and so it was. After judoka Or Sasson won a bronze medal at the Rio Olympics, Minister Regev congratulated him with hugs and kisses that left many viewers feeling uncomfortable.

Bring a Bossa // Screenshot

September 2016

And... Action!

In Regev's second year in office, all parties already knew that cultural ceremonies had turned from bland events with stale jokes and unappetizing refreshments into a heated political arena. The 2016 Ophir Awards ceremony was no exception, when rapper Tamer Nafar appeared on stage, incorporating a text from Arab-Israeli poet Mahmoud Darwish into his song. The minister, who knew about the performance, jumped from her seat and left the hall in protest, in coordination with the spokesperson who filmed the entire event. On the other hand, the artists left the hall when Regev took the stage to speak, and after all this activity, it is unclear if the butts are still uptight but they are certainly more chiseled.

In case the spokesperson was standing there // Screenshot

January 2017

Wait, wait, and another moment

In an interview a few months ago, Regev accidentally blurted out the phrase "I made revolutions that I think two ministers after me will not be able to fix." A great example of this occurred in January 2017 when she recounted no less than three breathtaking moments.

The first occurred when she attended the Jerusalem Film Festival where director Quentin Tarantino was a guest and was asked which of his films she liked the most, to which she responded with the look of a student caught cheating and said: "Wait, wait, wait, wait, wait, wait, wait, wait, I forgot his name." After the minister managed to remember the name of the film "Pulp Fiction," she was asked what she loved about it and she replied, "The whole, the whole, the whole story, the whole acting, the right touches, the complex system between men and women." Indeed, anyone who has seen the romantic comedy "Pulp Fiction" would surely agree how the relationships between men and women had the right touches.

The honored guest Tarantino was not particularly offended by the minister's words and since then has even managed to become a permanent resident of our country. But compared to Tarantino, someone who has never made Aliyah is basketball player Sean Dawson, who was born in Israel and lived there all his life, but when Regev met him, she complimented him on the fact that he already knows Hebrew, and thus the second brilliance of the month was born.

Hebrew is a difficult language // Screenshot

Most people would be satisfied with these two great achievements, but the work is plentiful and towards the end of the month, when criticism was published about the fact that the "Likudiada" event would take place on International Holocaust Remembrance Day, the minister defended the choice and said, "Thanks to the Likudiada, we cover International Holocaust Remembrance Day." 

May 2017

I was born in Cannes

Most stars arriving on the red carpet at the Cannes Film Festival try to go for a killer look, but Miri decided to go for a Western Wall look, when she arrived at the event in a dress designed for her by Aviad Herman, on which the skyline of ancient Jerusalem was painted. Even before the flashes lit up Miri's dress, "Eretz Nehederet" had already prepared to give Yuval Samo a similar dress, and all that remains is to wonder who wore it better.

On my head, my dress. Regev at Cannes

September 2017

It Takes Two to Foxtrot

The film "Foxtrot" starring Lior Ashkenazi won the Ophir Award, the Jury Prize at the Venice Film Festival, and was a strong contender for the Oscar, but the best thing that happened to the film was Minister Regev, whose public boycott of the work caused a huge audience to flock to cinemas to watch the controversial film. Regev herself admitted she had never seen the film and in our opinion, it is really a shame because she could definitely have enjoyed the right touches of the acting and the complex relationships between men and women.

• LA Times: "Lior Ashkenazi is a legend"

We recommend you watch, the touches there are something // Screenshot

January 2018

Own Goal

If you ever need to answer the question what is the definition of the term "dissonance," you could use as an example the video Regev published on her Facebook page in which she is surrounded by Beitar fans in the eastern stand and talks about how the game against Bnei Sakhnin is sportsmanlike and that sports will win, but as soon as the video ends, the fans around her shout, "Burn your village," while the minister stands in the middle of the crowd with a smile from ear to ear. Undoubtedly, sports won.

Undoubtedly, sports won // Photo: Alan Shiver

June 2018

from Messi to Messy

Not every day is a football team of the caliber of the Argentina national team led by legendary footballer Leo Messi expected to arrive in Israel, and most football fans would agree to accept the showcase of this team even if it were held on the remains of an Indian cemetery, but Minister Regev is not an ordinary fan and she began to impose a series of conditions in order to host Messi in the Holy Land. First, the original location in Haifa was not to her liking and she insisted on moving the game at a cost of 2.7 million shekels to Jerusalem. As if that wasn't enough, a widespread controversy arose over whether Messi would shake hands with the Prime Minister and the responsible minister. In the end, the game was canceled due to security concerns of the team, but many angry fans are still upset about the minister's interference in the game.

• Messi, and a lot of Miri Regev: the internet reacts to the cancellation

Minister Regev at the press conference after the cancellation // Photo: Gideon Markovich

December 2019

Who Got Grub?

It's not that the minister softened, but it seems the public got used to it. In 2019 she celebrated in Times Square at an event for the State of Israel and refused to let go of the microphone, in two government meetings she launched into long praises for the Prime Minister to the embarrassment of all present, again the Minister of Culture spoke out against Israeli works, this time targeting the film "Terrible Days" and the documentary about lawyer Leah Tzimel, and occasionally a recording of her calling the legal advisor "garbage" popped up, but it seems the threshold for excitement has risen. This, until Regev was caught making a racist remark referring to a member of Gideon Saar's party, when she said, "We will not let the Bukharans win," and although this is not the hardest statement made by the minister, hearing such a sentence from the woman who called "Garboz" to all racists still managed to surprise.

[tweet]
Summary: In honor of the end of Miri Regev's term as Minister of Culture and Sports, here is a review of the 15 most embarrassing moments of the outgoing minister. At the beginning of her term in June 2015, she was quoted saying that people in the cultural world are "tight-asses, hypocrites, schemers, ungrateful." She also stated that the fact that this is the position she received is probably a lesson that God wants to teach her. In September of that year, Regev was interviewed by "Israel Hayom" and declared that she plans to bring about a cultural revolution by changing the budget distribution of her ministry and reducing budgets for certain institutions and transferring them to others. This statement was perceived as a declaration of war on the "cultural elite" in Israel. In July 2016, the broadcasting corporation "Kan" was established in place of the Israel Broadcasting Authority. In response, many politicians spoke out against it. Regev chose the argument, "What is the point of the broadcasting corporation if we do not control it?" A month later, she drew attention again when she chose to participate in the show "Eyal Golan Calls You" and urged the public to watch the program. In response, the Knesset Ethics Committee ruled that Regev violated the prohibition against Knesset members promoting commercial content. Later, Regev also invited Golan to sing two songs at the "Crown of the East" festival for which he received tens of thousands of shekels. Another event that earned Regev public criticism that month was the way she greeted judoka Uri Sheshon with hugs and kisses after he won a bronze medal at the Rio Olympics. The next drama was in September 2016 at the Ophir Awards. In protest of the use of the words of poet Mahmoud Darwish in the song of rapper Tamer Nafar, Regev left the hall in protest. Correspondingly, the artists left the hall when Regev took the stage to speak. The next moment in which the minister shone was at the Jerusalem Film Festival when she met director Quentin Tarantino. He asked the minister what her favorite movie is. After a few moments of confusion, she said "Pulp Fiction," among other reasons due to "the complex relationship between men and women." In September 2017, Regev decided to boycott the film "Foxtrot," which she had not seen. In response, a large audience flocked to see the film to understand the reason for the boycott. Another embarrassing event for Regev occurred when she was photographed in January 2018 with Beitar Jerusalem fans and spoke about the match against Bnei Sakhnin being completely sporting. Meanwhile, the crowd of fans around her sang violent and racist songs against Arabs. Regev also became known for her intense intervention in the matters of the arrival of Argentina's famous football team. Ultimately, the team's arrival was canceled, and many fans were furious, claiming it was the minister's fault. In conclusion, a variety of controversial moments and statements by Regev: flattery towards the Prime Minister during meetings, opposition to other Israeli films, calling the Attorney General a derogatory name, and a racist call towards a member of her party, Saar.
Sentence in Summary: Another event that earned Regev public criticism that month was the way she greeted judoka Uri Sheshon with hugs and kisses after he won a bronze medal at the Rio Olympics
Best Match Sentences From Article: After judoka Or Sasson won a bronze medal at the Rio Olympics, Minister Regev congratulated him with hugs and kisses that left many viewers feeling uncomfortable

Article: Author "Cold Storage": "We are expected to see many natural disasters" David Koepp could not ignore the climate crisis when he came to write the thriller "Cold Storage," which centers around a deadly fungus threatening to destroy the world • "You can't write today without addressing global warming; the world is changing before our eyes," says the successful Hollywood screenwriter ("Jurassic Park," "Spider-Man") in an interview • He explains why his superhero suffers from back problems, and what winning advice he received from Michael Crichton "I wanted to write about monsters, but about microscopic monsters. We have enough big monsters. I did research and found fungi...". This is how David Koepp, author of "Cold Storage" (published by Kinneret-Zmora. Translated from English: Shaul Levin), describes his choice of the deadly heroine of his new book. Meet: Cordyceps, or by its scientific name Ophiocordyceps - a real, small, and cool fungus, whose way of spreading its spores is by invading the bodies of ants, taking over their brains, and forcing them to climb as high as possible on a tree in the jungle. After climbing to maximum height, the unfortunate ant will sprout tendrils from its head, which will burst and spread its spores over the jungle floor in a large radius, so that it can catch more ants - and so on. The story of the cordyceps, which has been dubbed the "zombie fungus," appears in full in David Attenborough's latest masterpiece series, "The Wonderful World," which was broadcast in Israel on Kan 11. Koepp expanded the capabilities of this real fungus by sending it to space, for an experiment in the first American space laboratory, "Skylab," which crashed on the shores of Australia and in the Indian Ocean in 1979. "The internet has greatly changed our use of information," Koepp says. "On one hand, we have access to a lot of knowledge, on the other hand, there is no one to interpret it for us. For a writer, this is an advantage. You are free to understand it and process it as you wish. And that's exactly what I did: I found the scientific basis on the internet, activated my imagination, wrote the book - and only then sent it to a microbiologist to point out and correct the gross scientific errors. 'You are allowed to read and laugh at me,' I told him, but to my surprise, he came back to me and said: 'The scientific parts are not terrible. Let's sit down and improve them.' I think I acted correctly. If I had met with experts before I wrote the book, my imagination would have been limited by scientific reality and I wouldn't have been able to activate it freely." A fun life for pay Although this is Koepp's debut novel (58), he is far from being anonymous. He is a star screenwriter, responsible for blockbusters like "Jurassic Park," "Mission: Impossible," "Spider-Man," and about 30 other films that most of us have seen. He has worked with the greatest directors in Hollywood, such as Steven Spielberg, Brian De Palma, and other giants, but he attributes his becoming a screenwriter to another blockbuster, the first in the "Indiana Jones" series - Raiders of the Lost Ark. "The film came out in 1981, I was 17 or 18," Koepp recalls. "I sat in the theater, and besides enjoying the film very much, the thought suddenly jumped into my mind that someone wrote the script and did it with great fun. For some reason, I could really see him doing it and enjoying it immensely. I told myself at that moment - that's it. That's the job I want. To have fun - and to get paid for it." What makes a very successful screenwriter change direction and become an author? "For me, it was a very liberating experience. The book allowed me to write, for example, three pages about what a certain character did three years before the events described. In a script, there is no such freedom. You can only write what the viewer will see or hear. You can only insert humor through a line that a character says. Screenwriting is a very specific art. As a writer, I felt completely free from limitations." Who are the authors that inspire you? "The genre I write in is science-based thriller. There aren't many authors writing in this genre, but the immediate association is Michael Crichton ("The Andromeda Strain," "Jurassic Park"), and indeed he doesn't have many competitors. His ideas were wonderful. 'Jurassic Park' is pure genius. But, if you want to be a successful author - good ideas are not enough. Crichton once told me: 'Writing is 10% inspiration and 90% sweat.' "Another person who helped me dispel the mystery around writing is Stephen King. Not only because he is a fantastic storyteller, and he creates believable characters that are easy to imagine in reality - I really felt like I knew the types, even the villains. I was especially impressed by his work process: he sits and writes every day for 4 hours, in the morning and in the early afternoon, and that's how he finishes his workday. But he does it every day!". Inspiration from a security guard Returning to "Cold Storage." You assign a very strange collection of characters - a struggling single mother, an aging agent, and a security guard possibly on the autism spectrum - the task of saving the world. "I started writing the book with the character of Tickey, the security guard. One day I was walking at 7 in the morning on a sweltering New York day, like only this city has, and in front of me was a young guy in his 20s, in a security guard uniform and with a very unhappy face. To me, he looked like someone who was going - or coming - from a job he really hates. And then I thought - but what if something terrible happens at that job, which he dislikes so much, and he tells himself: 'Maybe I have a bad job, but it's mine and I need to do what's right'? And I started writing simply a story about him. Later I asked myself: who else works with him? And that's how Naomi was born, who also has challenging life - existential anxieties, an ex-husband who mistreats her." And what about the retired agent, Roberto Diaz, who reminds me of Gandalf in "The Fellowship of the Ring" - the man who holds the knowledge? "I wrote his character as older, closer to my age, and thus I could give him some of my personal issues, like back pain. Besides that, I thought it would be very amusing to write a superhero character who, during the height of the action in the plot, is lying on his back." The book was published before the outbreak of the coronavirus and before the climatic shocks the world is experiencing today. In this respect, the book seems almost prophetic. "You can't write today about almost any topic without addressing global warming. This is the reality today. The world is changing before our eyes, and unfortunately, we will see more disasters as a result of this warming." You currently hold two successful careers. Which one would you like to continue with? "I love writing scripts because in a film you work with large teams, and that's great. On the other hand, I also love working alone, and writing books allows for freedom. Therefore, I will continue to engage in both professions. And here is a hot proof from the oven: in May-June next year, a second science-based thriller of mine will be published. It will be even more realistic, and therefore also scarier than 'Cold Storage'".
Summary: David Kope, an esteemed screenwriter in Hollywood, turns to writing thrillers in his new book "Cold Storage" which deals with a deadly fungus threatening the world. The fungus, called cordyceps, takes over the brains of ants and forces them to climb to great heights to disperse its spores. In his book, Kope takes the fungus's abilities a step further and raises the hypothesis of what would happen if it were sent into space. Kope recounts that he got the idea for the book from a young security guard and the sad expression he saw on the street. According to him, the process of writing a book is different from the process of writing a script in that it allows for much more creative freedom. Kope mentions that he drew a lot of inspiration from Michael Crichton and Stephen King, authors who write in the same genre. Although the book was published before the outbreak of the coronavirus pandemic and the climate shocks, it is seen as prophetic. Kope plans to continue working both on writing books and on writing scripts.
