Article: A black day for the competition The desire of the company Cellcom to acquire the young competitor that has "destroyed" profits for everyone is very bad news for an industry that has become competitive. November 5th is a black day for competition in the cellular industry. The desire of Cellcom to acquire the young competitor that has "destroyed" profits for everyone is very bad news for an industry that has become competitive. The Prime Minister, who currently serves as the Minister of Communications, would do well to instruct the CEO of his office to quickly remove the restrictions on the entry of additional new competitors into the field. This deal should not be left as a headache solely for the antitrust authority. Just as the state knows how to allocate land for free to those without housing, it should allocate frequencies for free to new players, provided they commit to establishing a new nationwide network. Only the entry of a few more new companies into the field will ensure that prices do not start to creep up again. Golan Telecom was an "unnatural" phenomenon for the cellular industry. It forced, as then-Minister of Communications Moshe Kahlon initiated, overnight competition. The public is grateful for introducing the previously unimaginable concept of "everything included". Who could have imagined such a thing until that point? But this deal is very bad news for the public. If it goes ahead, it will again be necessary to pave the way for the entry of new operators. We must not be lazy; we need to return and do this, and the address is the Ministry of Communications. Let it be clear - Cellcom and its shareholders should not be blamed - naturally, they want to return to higher profitability. Cellcom wants to improve its hold on the cellular industry. The reactions of capital market activists who smiled under their breath yesterday with the "we told you so" and the desire to see profits rise also reflect this. While it serves the public's pension, it will cost the public on the other side. Big profits are good news for the capital market, but the public itself? It pays twice. Once in management fees and once in increased rates. The government's task is to remove the barriers to the entry of competitors so that companies understand that competition is here to stay. The public needs competition, and for it to be sustainable, the state must remove the barriers it itself creates. • • • "The deal - a cry for generations" a blow to the cellular reform and consumers • Cellcom will acquire Golan Telecom for 1.17 billion shekels • The completion of the deal depends on regulatory approval • Serious concerns in the market over rising prices √ Kahlon: "A devastating blow to competition" • Erdan: "Impose fines and consider revoking the license" • Michael Golan promises: "The competition will continue" Anger in the political and economic system following the sale of Golan Telecom to Cellcom. Finance Minister Moshe Kahlon sent an urgent letter to the acting head of the antitrust authority, attorney Uri Schwartz, stating: "The Ministry of Finance opposes the approval of the deal. Granting approval for this acquisition will severely harm competition in the cellular market and lead to price increases." Kahlon further wrote that "in any scenario, Golan Telecom should not be allowed to be acquired by an existing operator, creating a cellular company with a market share of nearly 40%, while severely harming the existing competition in the market. The harm to competition in the cellular market will be a cry for generations. The merger of Golan and Cellcom is a serious mistake. Call rates will soar to hundreds of shekels a month instead of the tens of shekels the public pays today. Approval of the merger will take us back to days of lack of competition, and consumers will become victims of the move." The CEO of the Ministry of Finance, Shai Babad, said last night: "This is a deal that will take us back to the eve of the reform. We will reach a situation where Cellcom will become a company with a 40% market share. Customers do not disperse among the companies. Why should it (Cellcom) pay so much money? The answer is that once Cellcom takes the additional customers, it will have the options to raise rates and pass that on to consumers. The Finance Minister will not allow this." "Betraying customers" The Minister of Public Security, Gilad Erdan, who served in the previous government as Minister of Communications, also sent a particularly harsh letter yesterday to the acting head of the antitrust authority and the CEO of the Ministry of Communications, Shlomo Filber, demanding not to approve the deal. Erdan noted that "the new companies that won the tender received unprecedented conditions compared to the established companies, all to ensure they would introduce aggressive competition into a market that suffered from concentration and particularly high prices that burdened the public. If Golan Telecom does not meet the tender conditions and the company's owners, who invest billions of euros in other countries, are not willing to invest the required amount to meet the commitments Golan Telecom made, the right way is not to reward the company and sell it at a large profit to one of the existing companies. The appropriate decision would be to impose particularly large fines on it and consider revoking its license and selling it to a new cellular company that will enter the market in its place." Knesset member Yael German (Yesh Atid) is initiating an urgent discussion in the Economics Committee on the topic: "The Knesset is obligated to promote a discussion on a move with severe implications for competition in the sector and on the price of cellular services in Israel." The Ministry of Communications stated that "the ministry has not yet received the deal documents. Once the official documents reach us, the professionals in the ministry will examine them in full cooperation with the antitrust authority and will make an informed decision on the matter." Golan Telecom customers reacted sharply on the company's Facebook page to the company's announcement. Shira wrote: "Stop presenting it as if prices will remain the same, we are not stupid." The user Niv wrote: "This Frenchman took everyone for a ride." Tzahi summarized: "Shame and disgrace." Omer added that "this is a black day for Israeli communications, everyone will pay the price." Roy wrote: "We are back to being suckers." Amir: "Betraying customers." Yehuda referred to the owners: "You spit in the well you drink from." Amit summarized the general feeling: "You have erased your credibility." "Golan will remain independent" Yesterday morning it was reported that Cellcom is acquiring the competing company Golan Telecom for 1.17 billion shekels. If the deal is successfully completed, Cellcom will control about 37% of the market share in the country. The completion of the deal depends on approvals from the regulators - which may not necessarily be granted. Cellcom will need to obtain approval from the head of the antitrust authority and also the approval of the Ministry of Communications. According to estimates, as part of the deal structure, Cellcom will forgive Golan Telecom a debt of about 450 million shekels. For Cellcom, the integration processes of Golan Telecom as part of its business structure are simpler, as Golan Telecom "rode" on Cellcom's network. Since Golan has already dismantled most of its antennas, any other operator that would buy Golan would have to transfer 850,000 customers from Cellcom's network to another network, which is an expensive technological task. If the owners of Golan Telecom complete the deal, they may be required to return an amount of 270 million shekels to the state treasury, as part of the commitments they made when establishing it. Golan is expected to fight to avoid returning the amount to the state. "The competition will continue and Golan Telecom will remain the most aggressive company." This is what the company's owner Michael Golan said last night in a conversation from France. According to him, "the deal we made is good for the consumer, for Golan Telecom, and for competition. I will continue to work in the company. We will have a separate operation, with our own marketers, website, packages, and with our team, and we will retain all the flexibility we have." Last night, Golan sent a letter to the National Labor Federation stating that he is committed to entering into intensive negotiations until reaching a collective agreement that guarantees the rights of the employees. The head of the organization division at the National Labor Federation, Yaniv Mizrahi, said in response that "in light of the announcement, we decided as a gesture of goodwill to suspend the strike planned for tomorrow." Eduardo Elstein, chairman of IDB and Discount, noted yesterday that "this is a significant deal in the communications market that represents an important step for the company and the entire economy." Cellcom's CEO, Nir Stern, said: "We intend to keep Golan Telecom as an independent company, and we promise all Golan Telecom customers that we will honor all their existing agreements." Hazi Sternlicht and Ilan Gatniew.
Summary: Cellcom plans to acquire Golan Telecom for 1.17 billion shekels, a move that has sparked anger and sharp reactions in the political and economic system. Finance Minister Moshe Kahlon strongly opposes the deal, claiming that its approval will severely harm competition in the cellular market and return prices to high levels. The Minister of Public Security, Gilad Erdan, also opposes the deal and calls for heavy fines on Golan Telecom and considers revoking its license. Golan Telecom customers reacted with anger and feelings of betrayal following the acquisition announcement. Michael Golan, owner of Golan Telecom, claims that the deal is good for consumers and that Golan Telecom will continue to operate as an independent and competitive company. Cellcom, for its part, promises to honor all existing agreements with Golan Telecom customers and maintain Golan Telecom as an independent company. The completion of the deal is conditional on the approval of the Antitrust Authority and the Ministry of Communications, approvals that are not guaranteed. As part of the deal, Cellcom will forgive a debt of Golan Telecom to it, and it is possible that the owners of Golan Telecom will be required to return an amount of 270 million shekels to the state treasury. The acquisition raises serious concerns about harming competition and rising prices in the cellular market. Michael Golan, founder of Golan Telecom, promises that the company will remain competitive and aggressive despite the acquisition. The Ministry of Communications has not yet received the transaction documents and will examine them in cooperation with the Antitrust Authority before making a decision. Estimates are that as part of the deal, Cellcom will forgive a debt of Golan Telecom amounting to 450 million shekels. The National Union, which represented Golan Telecom employees, canceled the planned strike after Michael Golan committed to enter negotiations for a collective agreement. The deal has sparked numerous reactions on social media, with many Golan Telecom customers expressing concern about rising prices and harm to competition. Former Finance Minister Moshe Kahlon, who is considered the architect of the cellular reform, expressed strong opposition to the deal and claimed that it would severely harm competition. Market sources expressed concern that Cellcom's acquisition of Golan Telecom would lead to a sharp increase in cellular prices. Knesset member Yael German (Yesh Atid) called for an urgent discussion in the Knesset's Economic Committee following the deal. Eduardo Elstein, chairman of IDB and Discount, the controlling owner of Cellcom, noted that this is a significant and important deal for the communications market.
Sentence in Summary: Michael Golan, founder of Golan Telecom, promises that the company will remain competitive and aggressive despite the acquisition
Best Match Sentences From Article: "The competition will continue and Golan Telecom will remain the most aggressive company

Article:
Sex, Drugs, and Hospitalizations: The Dark Life of Matthew Perry

The appearance of the one who brought us Chandler Bing in the "Friends" reunion only strengthened the rumors about his poor health • For three decades he has struggled with addictions, going in and out of rehab centers and finding his name in tabloids under unflattering circumstances • The difficult story behind the light-hearted character from the beloved series, from then until today

No matter what Matthew Perry does in his professional life or how many upheavals and dramas he goes through in his private life, the character of Chandler Bing, the peak of his career - and in fact the alter ego that will accompany him forever whether he likes it or not - will always be remembered.

The contrast between his cynical yet funny and lively character in "Friends" and the man with the dark side that characterized his life outside the set is something that will probably accompany him forever, perhaps even under the auspices of fans and the media.

"I wanted to be famous so badly"

The role that changed his life forever, Chandler Bing, was given to Perry (51) in 1994 when he was 24 years old. "I was smoking out of my ears. I wanted to be famous so badly," he said in an interview with The New York Times in 2002 about the moment he learned he was cast in "Friends."

"The character of Chandler just jumped off the script, shook my hand, and said 'that's you,'" he recounted, revealing how almost instantly all his financial problems were solved. "I was on 'Friends' from age 24 to 34. I was at the hottest center of fame. We were everywhere back then," he said.

But as often happens when fame and money take over life, Perry found himself swept into a dangerous whirlwind from which he has not been able to extricate himself until today, as it seems, when every few years he experiences a setback and his name is splashed across tabloids under unflattering circumstances, including frequent announcements of his impending death.

In other interviews over the years, Perry admitted what had been known to fans for years. "I don't remember three seasons of Friends at all," he confessed when revealing how much his addiction to alcohol and drugs controlled his life during those years.

"When you're at the peak of success, you sometimes feel like you're in Disneyland," he said in an interview with The New York Times. "For me, it lasted about eight months. That feeling of 'look, I made it, I'm so excited, I have no problems in the world,' and then suddenly you realize you haven't achieved anything and it doesn't fill any hole in your life." The hole he speaks of is his addiction to alcohol and painkillers that intensified during the time of "Friends."

In 1997, Perry had a jet ski accident, and the doctor provided him with a prescription for Vicodin, which quickly led to a dangerous and life-threatening addiction. From there, the decline was rapid, and it was no longer possible to hide.

On screen, he continued to portray Chandler and tell cynical jokes, but the change in his appearance was noticeable as he lost many kilograms and took on a sickly and gaunt look. "It spiraled out of control," he admitted. "It was very unhealthy."

Attempts to help himself through rehab centers did not last, and over the years he went in and out of them.

I'll Be There for You

His five co-stars from "Friends," Matt LeBlanc, Jennifer Aniston, Courteney Cox, David Schwimmer, and Lisa Kudrow, as well as the producers and creators of the series, were aware of his condition, but as they revealed in interviews, there was almost nothing they could do except stand by.

"'Difficult' doesn't even begin to describe what was going on there," Kudrow (Phoebe Buffay) said in an interview with The New York Times. It is said that she was actually the only friend on set who dared to confront Perry with harsh words. "You're killing yourself, Matthew, and I don't want to get to a point where I watch you die," sources on set recounted about the difficult conversation she had with him.

Perry admitted that he knew how much everyone cared, but he was unable to do anything about it. "I wasn't ready to hear from anyone," he said. "You can't command someone to become sober. It has to come from you."

He added that no matter how low he had sunk, he had a rule he adhered to: not to arrive on set drunk. "Although I wouldn't drink on set, I did arrive with a severe hangover," he said in an interview with The New York Times. "It's terrible. You feel threatened but have to function normally and still be funny."

Matthew's addiction was so severe and took over his life during those years that he reached a real abyss. Katie Edwards, who was his girlfriend for a short time in the early 2000s, said in an interview that the addiction was so extreme that he exploited the fact that she was pregnant and enlisted her to get him drugs.

"We would open the bag and I would see that sometimes it was pills or cocaine, sometimes it was heroin and crack. I never knew what he would get," she said, adding that she preferred to help him and do it so he wouldn't have to encounter paparazzi or police.

The fact that he was so famous posed additional challenges for Perry on the road to recovery. "I'm a pretty private person," he said in an interview with The Hollywood Reporter. "But I starred in a show that 30 million people watched, so everyone knew exactly what I was going through. It was completely in the public eye. You can't deal with a drug problem for thirty years and expect to solve it in 28 days."

Over the years, Perry went in and out of rehab several more times, and as part of his recovery process, he even established a support center for addicts and recovering individuals in Malibu in 2013, which he sold in 2015, the same year he won the Phoenix Award for his help to addicts. "The best thing about me," he said then, "is that if today someone suffering from alcohol addiction comes to me and asks me to help him stop drinking, I can help him because I know how. That's the legacy I hope to leave behind," he said.

Ironically, at the height of his addiction in 2001, Perry had to juggle between filming "Friends" in Los Angeles and filming the movie "A Millionaire Who Donates First" in Dallas and had to move between the two cities.

"I was worried I was going to die the next day"

In February 2001, something changed in Perry, and one day while he was in his hotel room in Dallas, he decided to call his parents and ask for their help. "I didn't become sober because I wanted to," he admitted in an interview with The New York Times. "I became sober because I was worried I was going to die the next day." It was scary. I didn't want to die, but I am really grateful it got that bad because it made me more stubborn in the fight to try to recover."

Although it seemed on the surface that his condition had stabilized and despite the fact that he managed to stay in the public eye after "Friends" ended in 2004 thanks to movies and series he participated in, in reality, Perry continued to struggle with severe addiction issues, which this time led to a deterioration in his health and even to his hospitalization.

In 2018, gossip columns reported that Perry was rushed to the hospital due to a ruptured intestine, a medical condition that many claimed resulted from his long-term alcohol addiction, which also affected his liver function.

"You can't recover from what I'm suffering from overnight," he said in an interview with People magazine. "It's something you have to deal with day after day. I've had a lot of ups and downs and I've learned a lot from my failures."

Not Looking Good

As mentioned, over the years Perry continued to maintain a career on the big screen and on television (“Studio 60,” “Go On,” “Mr. Sunshine”), but despite small successes, it was clear that he was still struggling with his inner demons. Gossip columns enjoyed documenting him wandering the streets looking disheveled and reporting on various health issues he continued to suffer from.

About a year ago, when they began talking about the highly publicized reunion episode of "Friends," 17 years after it went off the air, it seemed that finally, it was Perry's time to return and receive the huge hug that the public so wanted to give him.

Filming of the episode was delayed by more than a year due to COVID, and when the teasers for the episode that aired at the end of May began to be distributed, it was again clear that Perry was the friend whom the years had treated the worst.

In clips from the filming, Perry appeared slumped and drained and spoke little. When he did speak, his speech was slow and unclear, leading many viewers and online users to express concern for his health. Some claimed he suffered a stroke, while others insisted he had again succumbed to alcohol and drug addiction. The consensus was one: Matthew Perry was in bad shape.

The producers of the reunion episode and close associates came to his defense, claiming that the reason for his poor appearance was a serious dental treatment he underwent just before filming that caused him great suffering, but viewers and fans were not buying it.

Meanwhile, a TikToker named Kate Harrelson claimed that Perry had hit on her last year on the dating app "Raya" when she was only 19, engaging in a conversation that included sexual references despite knowing exactly how old she was. Even before the story broke, gossip columns revealed that Perry had been expelled from the rehab program he participated in in 2019 because he was busy engaging in sexual texting with women on dating apps instead of focusing on his recovery process.

At his 50th birthday celebrations about a year ago, it was reported that Perry was seen spending time at a luxury hotel in New York, mostly staying in his room or sitting at the bar drinking.

As if that wasn't enough, Matthew reportedly broke up with his fiancée Molly Horowitz about a week ago after it seemed he had finally found the partner to build a home and family with.

The million-dollar question being asked today about Matthew Perry is what he is really going through, and whether he is once again on the fast track to crashing, just when it seemed he had finally managed to find some stability in his life. The answer is that as of now, no one really knows, and we can only continue to keep our fingers crossed for the beloved actor to find the happy and healthy place in his life.

Fame and money came, and Perry found himself swept into a dangerous whirlwind from which he has not managed to escape until today, it seems. His five co-stars from "Friends" as well as the producers and creators of the series were aware of his condition, but as they revealed in interviews, there was almost nothing they could do except stand by. Over the years, Perry went in and out of rehab several times, and as part of his recovery process, he even established a support center for addicts and recovering individuals in Malibu in 2013, which he sold in 2015, the same year he won the Phoenix Award for his help to addicts. The much-talked-about "Friends" reunion episode, which was postponed for more than a year due to COVID-19 about a year ago, revealed that Perry is the friend whom the years have treated the worst. In clips from the filming, Perry appeared downcast and burnt out and spoke little, which led many viewers and online users to express concern for his health. The producers of the reunion episode and close associates came to his defense, claiming that the reason for his poor appearance was a serious dental treatment he underwent just before filming that caused him great suffering, but viewers and fans were not buying it.

Meanwhile, a TikToker named Kate Harrelson claimed that Perry had hit on her last year on the dating app "Raya" when she was only 19, engaging in a conversation that included sexual references despite knowing exactly how old she was. Even before the story broke, gossip columns revealed that Perry had been expelled from the rehab program he participated in in 2019 because he was busy engaging in sexual texting with women on dating apps instead of focusing on his recovery process.

At his 50th birthday celebrations about a year ago, it was reported that Perry was seen spending time at a luxury hotel in New York, mostly staying in his room or sitting at the bar drinking.

As if that wasn't enough, Matthew reportedly broke up with his fiancée Molly Horowitz about a week ago after it seemed he had finally found the partner to build a home and family with.

The million-dollar question being asked today about Matthew Perry is what he is really going through, and whether he is once again on the fast track to crashing, just when it seemed he had finally managed to find some stability in his life. The answer is that as of now, no one really knows, and we can only continue to keep our fingers crossed for the beloved actor to find the happy and healthy place in his life.

His five co-stars from "Friends" as well as the producers and creators of the series were aware of his condition, but as they revealed in interviews, there was almost nothing they could do except stand by. Over the years, Perry went in and out of rehab several times, and as part of his recovery process, he even established a support center for addicts and recovering individuals in Malibu in 2013, which he sold in 2015, the same year he won the Phoenix Award for his help to addicts. Article:
The Oil Festival: Culinary Tours During the Holiday

While Hanukkah is a traditional holiday, the olive harvest festival is an agricultural celebration that sanctifies our deep connection to the land • the culinary route

Symbolically and very fittingly, the Israeli olive oil festival takes place every year around Hanukkah, the oil holiday. During this season, one can taste and enjoy the first harvest, the finest and most delicate virgin olive oil.

The harvest begins during the Sukkot holiday and ends with Hanukkah. While Hanukkah is a traditional holiday, the olive harvest festival is an agricultural celebration that sanctifies our deep connection to the land, its roots, and its produce.

Since the land is blessed with the seven species, including the olive, this agricultural holiday also has a touch of biblical and ancient tradition.

In ancient times, the people of Israel used to celebrate this period and bring pure olive oil to the Temple as an offering at the end of the harvest and the reception of the oil, while today, this first oil is a culinary treasure.

The taste of the first harvest is very delicate, soft, and round, and therefore its uses are more numerous, and it is loved by Israeli chefs. On this occasion, as every year, it is customary to present local oils in all their glory to culinary professionals.

Members of the Olive Branch Council gathered with great respect and honor for the first sip of the coveted oil. Uri Yogev, head of the olive branch at the Plant Council, Ehud Tsouriano, head of the Israeli tasting panel, Professor Zohar Keren, and chefs Einav Azaguri, Eran Zino, Orel Kimchi, Dudu Biton, Marcus Gershkovich, Asaf Doctor, Yonatan Borovitz, Kobi Becker, Yossi Ben Odys, Elias Matar, Tomer Tal, Tzachi Bokshester, and Avivit Priel. We accompanied them to hear the leading chefs' opinions on Israeli olive oil.

Additionally, in honor of Hanukkah, the olive branch offers a variety of activities and events related to olive oil. This way, one can meet the growers, producers, taste fresh and high-quality local olive oil, listen to lectures, and of course, taste special dishes adorned with wonderful Israeli olive oil.

Harvesting Kibbutz Magal – A Branch with Roots

Kibbutz Magal offers explanations and myth-busting about olive oil, tastings of various varieties, watching a short film – "From the Field to the Bottle," visiting the orchards, and receiving explanations about the different growing methods (depending on the weather).

When: During the days of Hanukkah, Sunday-Thursday: 08:00-16:00, Friday 09:00-13:00

Where: The visitor center at Kibbutz Magal, Emek Hefer

How much: Admission is free

Advance coordination by phone: 04-6287928 / on the website: www.masik.co.il

Celebrations of the First Harvest in Selected Restaurants

Selected restaurants will host the Achia farm for a special meal. The olive oil from Achia farm, "First Harvest," is released in a limited edition. It has flavors and aromas characteristic of the beginning of the harvest, where the values of taste and health are at their peak.

December 16-18, 2019, at Angelica Restaurant, George Washington 4, Jerusalem. Phone 02-6230056

December 16-18, 2019, at West Side Restaurant, Royal Beach Hotel, Hayarkon 19, Tel Aviv, Phone: 03-7405054

Until December 31, 2019, Waldorf Astoria Hotel, Gershon Agron 26, Jerusalem, Phone: 02-5423333

Galilee Olive Press // Photo: Omer Eilat

Achia Farm, Shiloh

A tour at the olive press and visitor center in Shiloh and watching the harvest in one of the most beautiful places in the country, where the olive trees and vineyards of Achia farm are planted. Alongside the olive groves that span 350 dunams, Achia farm is a modern olive press, one of the largest in the country, chosen as one of the 25 best olive presses in the world.

When: Days of Hanukkah – Sunday to Thursday, 08:30-15:30

Where: Achia Farm, Shiloh

Phone: 02-9401313 m-achiya@m-achiya.co.il

Geshur Land, Kibbutz Geshur

An experiential tour that introduces you to the fascinating world of Geshur Land olive oil. The visit is intended for the whole family and includes watching the advanced process of extracting quality oil, watching the film "The Story of Geshur Land Oil," and of course, tasting a variety of varietal oils while learning about the uses and unique aroma of each variety. There is also an option for a tour of the orchards to see the harvester in action during the harvest season.

When: Days of Hanukkah – Sunday to Thursday, 08:00-16:00, Friday 10:00-14:00

Where: Geshur Olive Press, Golan Heights

By prior arrangement by phone: 04-6764036

Olive Oil Celebrations in Nesher, Nesher Cultural Center

A workshop on drumming on olive oil cans, floor games following the "Miracle of the Oil Jug," an olive pickling workshop, and selling fresh quality olive oil and a candle lighting ceremony.

When: Wednesday, December 25, 2019, 17:00-19:00

Where: Cultural Center, Nesher

Cortina // Photo: Roni Heilman from Heilman Farm

Galilee Olive Press, Bethlehem of Galilee

The olive press offers tastings of oils from five olive varieties, wine tastings, and goat cheese. The café serves special dishes combining the five oils, background music, and a magical view.

When: Friday and Saturday, December 27-28, 2019, from 10:00-16:00

Where: Galilee Olive Press, Bethlehem of Galilee

Olive Oil Tasting Workshop

A workshop that teaches how to identify positive and negative taste and aroma characteristics and how to determine the quality level of olive oil using them. A blind tasting of several oils and tips for various and unconventional culinary uses with olive oils from different varieties.

When: Friday, December 27 at 12:00, and Saturday, December 28 at 11:00.

Where: Galilee Olive Press, Bethlehem of Galilee

How much: 25 shekels per participant (participation is limited to ages 12 and up).

Phone: 052-6154411

Noam's Artichoke, Nir Banim

A guided walking tour in the olive orchards of the past, present, and future of the olive oil industry, focusing on health and nutritional aspects, a special vegetarian brunch based on artichokes and fresh quality olive oil, and lighting candles in an artichoke menorah.

When: Every Friday and Saturday 10:00-16:00

Where: Noam's Artichoke, Nir Banim

Phone: 054-2278637

Lukanda Restaurant Nazareth Yogurt Dish with Shishbarak and Kubba with Mint, Garlic, and Olive Oil // Photo: Efi Gabai

Goren Olive Press and Winery, near Goren Park

The olive press has won several gold medals in international competitions and also first place in the world for oils produced at the olive press. Three types of extra virgin olive oil can be purchased. Additionally, red wine, liqueurs, raw tahini, olives, and olive oil cosmetics can be purchased.

When: Sundays-Thursdays and Saturdays 09:00-16:00

Where: Goren Olive Press and Winery on the northern road between the Keziv Stream and the Betzet Stream near Goren Park

Phone: 052-8854255 (Yaakov)

Mobile Photography Workshop Under the Nose with Natali Tamir

Ancient olive trees that tell stories through the mobile lens with explanations and tastings of olive oils at the olive press. Learn about perspective reversal, various techniques to leverage creativity and observation, creating something from nothing, motion photography, emotion, basic editing, and more. The workshop provides a variety of techniques and tools for mobile photography and is suitable for all levels, no prior knowledge is required.

When: Friday, December 27, 2019, from 10:00-13:30

Where: Rish Lakish Olive Press, Zippori

How much: 350 shekels, a 10% discount will be given to couples (subject to a minimum of 8 participants)

To register: https://did.li/peleworkshpo / WhatsApp message: Natali 052-6682811

Legacy Hotel Rooftop Restaurant Nazareth // Photo: Fata Shamiya

A special cooking workshop for Hanukkah including a tour of the olive press and winery of Zippori

Explanations about olive oil and tastings, a special Latin cooking workshop for Hanukkah where corn empanadas, gnocchi with jalapeño, salads in avocado dressing, and for dessert, Chilean calzones will be prepared. On the day of the workshop, there will also be explanations and tastings of wine at the winery of Zippori.

When: Friday, December 27, 09:30-13:30

Where: Rish Lakish Olive Press, Zippori

How much: 300 shekels per participant (subject to a minimum of 5 participants)

Vicky: 052-6966286

Heilman Farm, Moshav Nov, Golan Heights

Meet at the Heilman farm orchard in Moshav Nov for a tour among the orchards of plum, apple, and nectarine trees, where there is a modern olive grove working with mechanical harvesting, where a brief explanation about olive cultivation, types of oils, and more will be given.

When: Hanukkah days, Sunday to Thursday: 08:00-17:00, Fridays 08:00-14:00

Where: Heilman Farm Orchard, Moshav Nov

By prior arrangement, Avia: 052-2983325

Hanukkah at Tura Winery

A tour at Tura Winery in Rachelim including a tasting of three wines, olive oil, and a full guide combined with the story of the winery. The olive oil is a blend of three varieties: Picual, Barnea, and Arbequina.

Where: Tura Winery, Rachelim

How much: 25 shekels per person (with a tour reservation - a free donut for each visitor)

By prior arrangement: 02-6508882 | 052-7966613

Rooftop Restaurant, Mamilla Hotel, Jerusalem

Chef Simon Shapiro will serve at the Rooftop Restaurant of the Jerusalem lifestyle hotel Mamilla a variety of dishes with Israeli olive oil such as a sea fish tartare dish, fermented hot peppers, preserved lemon, and coriander on eggplant carpaccio and Persian lemon seasoned with olive oil powder (72 shekels), a confit sea bass dish in olive oil, a chickpea stew, roasted peppers, sun-dried cherry tomatoes in olive oil, harissa, kale, and grilled lemon (132 shekels) and other dishes with first harvest olive oil.

Rooftop Restaurant, Mamilla Hotel, Jerusalem. Phone: 02-5482230

Specials of First Harvest Olive Oil by Chef Elias Matar at Lukanda Restaurant, Ramada Olivie Hotel, Nazareth

In Chef Elias Matar's kitchen, olive oil is a star all year round, but for the holidays, the harvest, Christmas, and Hanukkah, many changing special dishes will be served, including dishes with first harvest olive oil, such as:

Albash and Asakro: Yogurt stew with shishbarak and kubba rich in dry mint, garlic, and olive oil.

Winter 2019 Baladi Stew (okra, purslane, and mallow in olive oil) with lamb chops from the taboon and more.

Lukanda Restaurant, Zionism Road 29, Ramada Olivie Hotel complex, Nazareth. Phone 04-6130351

Cooking Workshop with Olive Oil at Luna Restaurant

A two-hour cooking workshop, preparing purslane as an original Nazareth dish based on high-quality olive oil, chicken thighs with onion and sumac in olive oil on a dough base in the taboon, as well as macaroni in olive oil.

When: Monday, December 23, 2019, at 09:00

Where: Luna Bistro Restaurant, Nazareth. Phone: 054-4889818

"Patoura" Olive Oil - Celebrations of "First Harvest" at Rosinka Restaurant

Rosinka Restaurant offers a special counter for tastings of "Patoura" olive oil from the first harvest, selling bottles and incorporating the fresh oil into selected dishes on the restaurant menu.

Where: Lilienblum 41, Tel Aviv, Phone: 03-6344822

Article:
The Oil Festival: Culinary Tours During the Holiday

While Hanukkah is a traditional holiday, the olive harvest festival is an agricultural celebration that sanctifies our deep connection to the land • the culinary route

Symbolically and very fittingly, the Israeli olive oil festival takes place every year around Hanukkah, the oil holiday. During this season, one can taste and enjoy the first harvest, the finest and most delicate virgin olive oil.

The harvest begins during the Sukkot holiday and ends with Hanukkah. While Hanukkah is a traditional holiday, the olive harvest festival is an agricultural celebration that sanctifies our deep connection to the land, its roots, and its produce.

Since the land is blessed with the seven species, including the olive, this agricultural holiday also has a touch of biblical and ancient tradition.

In ancient times, the people of Israel used to celebrate this period and bring pure olive oil to the Temple as an offering at the end of the harvest and the reception of the oil, while today, this first oil is a culinary treasure.

The taste of the first harvest is very delicate, soft, and round, and therefore its uses are more numerous, and it is loved by Israeli chefs. On this occasion, as every year, it is customary to present local oils in all their glory to culinary professionals.

Members of the Olive Branch Council gathered with great respect and honor for the first sip of the coveted oil. Uri Yogev, head of the olive branch at the Plant Council, Ehud Tsouriano, head of the Israeli tasting panel, Professor Zohar Keren, and chefs Einav Azaguri, Eran Zino, Orel Kimchi, Dudu Biton, Marcus Gershkovich, Asaf Doctor, Yonatan Borovitz, Kobi Becker, Yossi Ben Odys, Elias Matar, Tomer Tal, Tzachi Bokshester, and Avivit Priel. We accompanied them to hear the leading chefs' opinions on Israeli olive oil.

Additionally, in honor of Hanukkah, the olive branch offers a variety of activities and events related to olive oil. This way, one can meet the growers, producers, taste fresh and high-quality local olive oil, listen to lectures, and of course, taste special dishes adorned with wonderful Israeli olive oil.

Harvesting Kibbutz Magal – A Branch with Roots

Kibbutz Magal offers explanations and myth-busting about olive oil, tastings of various varieties, watching a short film – "From the Field to the Bottle," visiting the orchards, and receiving explanations about the different growing methods (depending on the weather).

When: During the days of Hanukkah, Sunday-Thursday: 08:00-16:00, Friday 09:00-13:00

Where: The visitor center at Kibbutz Magal, Emek Hefer

How much: Admission is free

Advance coordination by phone: 04-6287928 / on the website: www.masik.co.il

Celebrations of the First Harvest in Selected Restaurants

Selected restaurants will host the Achia farm for a special meal. The olive oil from Achia farm, "First Harvest," is released in a limited edition. It has flavors and aromas characteristic of the beginning of the harvest, where the values of taste and health are at their peak.

December 16-18, 2019, at Angelica Restaurant, George Washington 4, Jerusalem. Phone 02-6230056

December 16-18, 2019, at West Side Restaurant, Royal Beach Hotel, Hayarkon 19, Tel Aviv, Phone: 03-7405054

Until December 31, 2019, Waldorf Astoria Hotel, Gershon Agron 26, Jerusalem, Phone: 02-5423333

Galilee Olive Press // Photo: Omer Eilat

Achia Farm, Shiloh

A tour at the olive press and visitor center in Shiloh and watching the harvest in one of the most beautiful places in the country, where the olive trees and vineyards of Achia farm are planted. Alongside the olive groves that span 350 dunams, Achia farm is a modern olive press, one of the largest in the country, chosen as one of the 25 best olive presses in the world.

When: Days of Hanukkah – Sunday to Thursday, 08:30-15:30

Where: Achia Farm, Shiloh

Phone: 02-9401313 m-achiya@m-achiya.co.il

Geshur Land, Kibbutz Geshur

An experiential tour that introduces you to the fascinating world of Geshur Land olive oil. The visit is intended for the whole family and includes watching the advanced process of extracting quality oil, watching the film "The Story of Geshur Land Oil," and of course, tasting a variety of varietal oils while learning about the uses and unique aroma of each variety. There is also an option for a tour of the orchards to see the harvester in action during the harvest season.

When: Days of Hanukkah – Sunday to Thursday, 08:00-16:00, Friday 10:00-14:00

Where: Geshur Olive Press, Golan Heights

By prior arrangement by phone: 04-6764036

Olive Oil Celebrations in Nesher, Nesher Cultural Center

A workshop on drumming on olive oil cans, floor games following the "Miracle of the Oil Jug," an olive pickling workshop, and selling fresh quality olive oil and a candle lighting ceremony.

When: Wednesday, December 25, 2019, 17:00-19:00

Where: Cultural Center, Nesher

Cortina // Photo: Roni Heilman from Heilman Farm

Galilee Olive Press, Bethlehem of Galilee

The olive press offers tastings of oils from five olive varieties, wine tastings, and goat cheese. The café serves special dishes combining the five oils, background music, and a magical view.

When: Friday and Saturday, December 27-28, 2019, from 10:00-16:00

Where: Galilee Olive Press, Bethlehem of Galilee

Olive Oil Tasting Workshop

A workshop that teaches how to identify positive and negative taste and aroma characteristics and how to determine the quality level of olive oil using them. A blind tasting of several oils and tips for various and unconventional culinary uses with olive oils from different varieties.

When: Friday, December 27 at 12:00, and Saturday, December 28 at 11:00.

Where: Galilee Olive Press, Bethlehem of Galilee

How much: 25 shekels per participant (participation is limited to ages 12 and up).

Phone: 052-6154411

Noam's Artichoke, Nir Banim

A guided walking tour in the olive orchards of the past, present, and future of the olive oil industry, focusing on health and nutritional aspects, a special vegetarian brunch based on artichokes and fresh quality olive oil, and lighting candles in an artichoke menorah.

When: Every Friday and Saturday 10:00-16:00

Where: Noam's Artichoke, Nir Banim

Phone: 054-2278637

Lukanda Restaurant Nazareth Yogurt Dish with Shishbarak and Kubba with Mint, Garlic, and Olive Oil // Photo: Efi Gabai

Goren Olive Press and Winery, near Goren Park

The olive press has won several gold medals in international competitions and also first place in the world for oils produced at the olive press. Three types of extra virgin olive oil can be purchased. Additionally, red wine, liqueurs, raw tahini, olives, and olive oil cosmetics can be purchased.

When: Sundays-Thursdays and Saturdays 09:00-16:00

Where: Goren Olive Press and Winery on the northern road between the Keziv Stream and the Betzet Stream near Goren Park

Phone: 052-8854255 (Yaakov)

Mobile Photography Workshop Under the Nose with Natali Tamir

Ancient olive trees that tell stories through the mobile lens with explanations and tastings of olive oils at the olive press. Learn about perspective reversal, various techniques to leverage creativity and observation, creating something from nothing, motion photography, emotion, basic editing, and more. The workshop provides a variety of techniques and tools for mobile photography and is suitable for all levels, no prior knowledge is required.

When: Friday, December 27, 2019, from 10:00-13:30

Where: Rish Lakish Olive Press, Zippori

How much: 350 shekels, a 10% discount will be given to couples (subject to a minimum of 8 participants)

To register: https://did.li/peleworkshpo / WhatsApp message: Natali 052-6682811

Legacy Hotel Rooftop Restaurant Nazareth // Photo: Fata Shamiya

A special cooking workshop for Hanukkah including a tour of the olive press and winery of Zippori

Explanations about olive oil and tastings, a special Latin cooking workshop for Hanukkah where corn empanadas, gnocchi with jalapeño, salads in avocado dressing, and for dessert, Chilean calzones will be prepared. On the day of the workshop, there will also be explanations and tastings of wine at the winery of Zippori.

When: Friday, December 27, 09:30-13:30

Where: Rish Lakish Olive Press, Zippori

How much: 300 shekels per participant (subject to a minimum of 5 participants)

Vicky: 052-6966286

Heilman Farm, Moshav Nov, Golan Heights

Meet at the Heilman farm orchard in Moshav Nov for a tour among the orchards of plum, apple, and nectarine trees, where there is a modern olive grove working with mechanical harvesting, where a brief explanation about olive cultivation, types of oils, and more will be given.

When: Hanukkah days, Sunday to Thursday: 08:00-17:00, Fridays 08:00-14:00

Where: Heilman Farm Orchard, Moshav Nov

By prior arrangement, Avia: 052-2983325

Hanukkah at Tura Winery

A tour at Tura Winery in Rachelim including a tasting of three wines, olive oil, and a full guide combined with the story of the winery. The olive oil is a blend of three varieties: Picual, Barnea, and Arbequina.

Where: Tura Winery, Rachelim

How much: 25 shekels per person (with a tour reservation - a free donut for each visitor)

By prior arrangement: 02-6508882 | 052-7966613

Rooftop Restaurant, Mamilla Hotel, Jerusalem

Chef Simon Shapiro will serve at the Rooftop Restaurant of the Jerusalem lifestyle hotel Mamilla a variety of dishes with Israeli olive oil such as a sea fish tartare dish, fermented hot peppers, preserved lemon, and coriander on eggplant carpaccio and Persian lemon seasoned with olive oil powder (72 shekels), a confit sea bass dish in olive oil, a chickpea stew, roasted peppers, sun-dried cherry tomatoes in olive oil, harissa, kale, and grilled lemon (132 shekels) and other dishes with first harvest olive oil.

Rooftop Restaurant, Mamilla Hotel, Jerusalem. Phone: 02-5482230

Specials of First Harvest Olive Oil by Chef Elias Matar at Lukanda Restaurant, Ramada Olivie Hotel, Nazareth

In Chef Elias Matar's kitchen, olive oil is a star all year round, but for the holidays, the harvest, Christmas, and Hanukkah, many changing special dishes will be served, including dishes with first harvest olive oil, such as:

Albash and Asakro: Yogurt stew with shishbarak and kubba rich in dry mint, garlic, and olive oil.

Winter 2019 Baladi Stew (okra, purslane, and mallow in olive oil) with lamb chops from the taboon and more.

Lukanda Restaurant, Zionism Road 29, Ramada Olivie Hotel complex, Nazareth. Phone 04-6130351

Cooking Workshop with Olive Oil at Luna Restaurant

A two-hour cooking workshop, preparing purslane as an original Nazareth dish based on high-quality olive oil, chicken thighs with onion and sumac in olive oil on a dough base in the taboon, as well as macaroni in olive oil.

When: Monday, December 23, 2019, at 09:00

Article: Cheap and tasty, up to 50 Alongside the excitement and festivity that envelop the preparations for the holiday meals, the challenge of cooking a holiday meal is often accompanied by significant financial pressures • We accepted the challenge and assembled a diverse feast from a carefully curated basket, costing less than 50 shekels per diner • The result - on the table the preparations for the holiday meal involve a considerable amount of pressure. Besides the planning and execution, which should not be underestimated in their importance and the level of stress they provide, the expenses borne by the hosts can amount to hundreds of shekels, and this fact can even cause me to lose my composure. I am a naturally planned and organized person, but when I cook, I cook with all my heart, thoroughly and completely. I have never checked the grocery bill, the butcher, or the fishmonger. Ahead of Rosh Hashanah, I took on a challenging task and immediately rejoiced in it: I decided to cook a festive, colorful, and incredibly tasty holiday table, entirely composed of a basket of products that does not exceed 50 shekels per diner. Moreover, I decided to create a diverse menu from which a wonderful meal can easily be assembled. After hours of planning, scribbling lists and tasks in a notebook, calculated and meticulous shopping, and frantic and enjoyable cooking, here are the results for the glory of the holiday and in honor of a sweet, wonderful, and tasty year. Fish patties in carrot sauce Ingredients (for about 18 patties, depending on size): • 500 grams of white fish fillet without skin and bones, cut into large cubes • 1/2 cup breadcrumbs • 1 egg • 1/2 chopped onion • 1/2 lemon, peeled and seeded • 2 cloves of garlic • 1 tablespoon grated ginger • 4 chopped green onion stalks (the white part and the green part) • 1 cup mixed parsley, mint, dill, and cilantro leaves • 4 tablespoons olive oil • Salt and pepper to taste For coating: • 1 cup breadcrumbs • Oil for frying For the sauce: • 8 carrots or 2 cups of fresh carrot juice • 2 sliced garlic cloves • 4 green onions halved lengthwise Preparation method: 1. Grind all the ingredients in a food processor to a smooth paste and transfer to the refrigerator for 15 minutes until the mixture is easy to work with. 2. With clean and slightly wet hands, shape round balls. Coat each ball in breadcrumbs and fry in hot oil (not boiling) for about 4 minutes on each side until the patties are golden. 3. Before serving, squeeze carrot juice and heat in a wide pan with sliced garlic and green onion until the mixture bubbles. 4. Season with salt and pepper and add the patties to warm with the sauce. Chicken liver with apples Ingredients (for 6 servings): • 600 grams of cleaned chicken liver • 2 heaping tablespoons of olive oil, or goose fat • 1 shot of Calvados or brandy (60 ml) • 2 apples cut into wedges • Salt and ground pepper to taste • Pomegranate seeds for garnish Preparation method: 1. Heat the olive oil or goose fat in a wide pan and season the livers. 2. Add the livers and apples to the pan and brown them on both sides for about 3 minutes on each side. 3. Add the liquor to the hot pan and cook for another minute. 4. Serve garnished with pomegranate seeds. Carrot salad Ingredients: • 6 peeled carrots cut into slices • 3 crushed garlic cloves • 2 tablespoons pickled lemon • 1/4 cup olive oil • 2 tablespoons honey • 1 teaspoon smoked paprika • 1 tablespoon crushed coriander seeds • 1/2 teaspoon sweet paprika • Atlantic salt to taste Preparation method: 1. Arrange all the ingredients in a roasting pan. 2. Cover with aluminum foil and roast at medium heat (180 degrees) for about 30 minutes. 3. Cool, adjust seasoning, garnish with coriander leaves, and serve. Honey roasted chicken Ingredients: • 1 chicken weighing about 1.5 kg • 2 liters of water • 1/2 cup salt • 1/4 cup sugar For the spice mixture: • 1/4 cup olive oil • 1 teaspoon paprika • 2 tablespoons silan • 2 tablespoons honey • 2 tablespoons Worcestershire sauce (or 1 tablespoon soy sauce) • Ground black pepper • 1 lemon sliced thinly For the vegetable mixture: • 1 kg of small potatoes • 4 peeled carrots cut lengthwise • 2 peeled onions cut into wedges • 8 whole garlic cloves • Thyme sprigs Preparation method: 1. Dissolve salt and sugar in 2 liters of water. Transfer the chicken to a container that fits its size and pour the solution over it until covered. 2. Soak in the refrigerator for at least 4 hours, preferably overnight. 3. Mix the olive oil with all the spices until a smooth and uniform mixture is obtained. 4. Remove the chicken from the water and pat it dry with paper towels. 5. Gently and thoroughly massage the chicken with the spice paste, also under the skin gently, without tearing. Arrange lemon slices under the skin on all sides. 6. In a pan suitable for the size of the chicken (not too wide and large), evenly spread the potatoes, carrots, onions, and garlic. 7. Place the chicken on top of the vegetables and roast at high heat (250 degrees) for 10 minutes on each side. 8. Lower the oven temperature to medium (180 degrees) and roast for 40 minutes, with the breast side facing the potatoes and the thigh side facing up (this prevents the lean breast from drying out too much). Add the tea, coffee, and spices to the boiling water and steep for 5 minutes. Strain out the tea bag. 2. In a mixer with a paddle attachment, mix all the cake ingredients with the spiced water until a uniform batter is obtained. 3. Grease two loaf pans and pour the batter evenly. 4. Arrange fan-shaped slices of apple in the pans. 5. Bake in a low oven (150 degrees) for about 50 minutes, until a toothpick inserted in the center of the cake comes out dry. Up to 50 shekels per diner Apple, pear, and guava compote Ingredients (for 4 servings): • 2 pears sliced thinly • 2 apples sliced thinly • 2 guavas sliced thinly • 1/2 cup raisins • 1 vanilla bean • 4 tablespoons honey • 2 tea bags of pomegranate infusion (optional) • Water to cover the pot • 1/2 cup sugar Preparation method: 1. Article: Cheap and tasty, up to 50 Alongside the excitement and festivity that envelop the preparations for the holiday meals, the challenge of cooking a holiday meal is often accompanied by significant financial pressures • We accepted the challenge and assembled a diverse feast from a carefully curated basket, costing less than 50 shekels per diner • The result - on the table the preparations for the holiday meal involve a considerable amount of pressure. Besides the planning and execution, which should not be underestimated in their importance and the level of stress they provide, the expenses borne by the hosts can amount to hundreds of shekels, and this fact can even cause me to lose my composure. I am a naturally planned and organized person, but when I cook, I cook with all my heart, thoroughly and completely. I have never checked the grocery bill, the butcher, or the fishmonger. Ahead of Rosh Hashanah, I took on a challenging task and immediately rejoiced in it: I decided to cook a festive, colorful, and incredibly tasty holiday table, entirely composed of a basket of products that does not exceed 50 shekels per diner. Moreover, I decided to create a diverse menu from which a wonderful meal can easily be assembled. After hours of planning, scribbling lists and tasks in a notebook, calculated and meticulous shopping, and frantic and enjoyable cooking, here are the results for the glory of the holiday and in honor of a sweet, wonderful, and tasty year. Grind all the ingredients in a food processor to a smooth paste and transfer to the refrigerator for 15 minutes until the mixture is easy to work with. 2. Before serving, squeeze carrot juice and heat in a wide pan with sliced garlic and green onion until the mixture bubbles. 4. Season with salt and pepper and add the patties to warm with the sauce. Article: It is forbidden to use information "before everyone" A large public is invested in public companies and has ownership over them • Using internal information before it is reported to the public is a violation of the law • An explanation of the internal information case and Minister Katz The suspicions arising in the current case, to which the names of Minister Chaim Katz and financial advisor Moti Ben Ari are linked, relate to a large transaction that took place between two real estate companies. The information that was known on the eve of that transaction to the relevant parties in the company is what is defined in legal language as "inside information". To understand what this is about, one must take a step back and explain the field of public companies. These companies are special companies whose shares or bonds are traded on the Tel Aviv Stock Exchange. These are companies in which a large public is invested, and it has a share of ownership over them. These companies are subject to special obligations, much more severe than those applicable to private companies. The guiding principle in this matter is the principle of fair disclosure, and the one responsible for its preservation - the "police" of the capital market - is the Securities Authority. When a public company experiences a significant event, such as a large agreement, a merger with another company, the sale of a large operation, a huge transaction, etc., the company is obliged to report to the general public about the event, which is done through a special system of the Securities Authority called MAGNA (Electronic Fair Disclosure System). The reports of that system are also distributed through a system of the stock exchange called MA'AH. Anyone who knows about a significant event in the life of the public company, before it is reported to the general public, actually holds "inside information". If that person uses inside information, meaning hurries to buy or sell securities of the company, he is violating the law. People who work or are connected to the company are defined as "insiders", and they are subject to additional duties of care. It is important to note that anyone who reveals this important information before everyone, meaning holds "inside information" and acts based on that information, is violating the law and may face prosecution. According to suspicions, in the case in question, inside information was used regarding that large transaction between Airport City and Nitsba, before it was reported to the public. An alleged purchase of securities by those people, if they knew the information in question "before everyone", is of course considered a violation of the law. • • • Suspicion: Minister Chaim Katz used inside information - and made a large profit Katz and financial advisor Moti Ben Ari were investigated by the Securities Authority • Suspected of purchasing shares of Nitsba, controlled by businessman Kobi Maimon, before a merger transaction with Airport City • Katz's associates: "We are sure that the picture will clarify for the better" A new case at the center of which is suspicion of using inside information that led to transactions in securities. Welfare Minister Chaim Katz was detained and questioned the day before yesterday by the Securities Authority, on suspicion of allegedly using inside information - one of the most serious offenses under the Securities Law. Minister Katz was not summoned for further questioning and was not arrested, and his associates said that they are "sure that the picture will clarify for the better in the coming days". As part of the case, financial advisor Moti Ben Ari, who is close to businessman Kobi Maimon, who has business ties with Katz, was also detained and questioned. The court released Ben Ari under restrictive conditions and ordered him to deposit his passport with the Securities Authority for 180 days. In addition, a travel ban was issued against him until November 16, and he must deposit a bank guarantee of one million shekels by the end of the month. "My client denies the suspicions and denies the allegations against him," said Ben Ari's lawyer, "but he agrees to the release conditions." The Securities Authority confirmed that Katz was questioned at the offices of the Securities Authority on suspicion of offenses related to the use of inside information and that he cooperated in his investigation. The authority stated that "the investigation was approved by the Attorney General and was accompanied by the Tel Aviv District Prosecutor's Office for Taxation and Economics." At the center of the case is a merger transaction between the companies Nitsba and Airport City, which was reported to the Securities Authority on July 5, 2015, a period during which Katz served as Minister of Welfare. The transaction, according to the appendix to the request for his release under restrictive conditions submitted by the Securities Authority, was known to him due to his being a financial advisor to Airport City and Nitsba since 1999. "The subject of this investigation concerns the suspicion of offenses related to the use of inside information that were committed by several different parties regarding the details of negotiations conducted by Airport with Nitsba," it was written in the request. It was also claimed that "as a result of this illegal activity, the suspects Katz and Ben Ari made illegal financial profits totaling approximately 290,000 shekels." Ben Ari is suspected of purchasing shares of Nitsba before the transaction was published and "despite an explicit prohibition from the company to do so. In addition, it is suspected that Ben Ari transferred information to Katz, using inside information." On the day of the report on the transaction, Nitsba's shares soared and continued to rise in the following days. In order to make a profit of 290,000 shekels, it was necessary to purchase shares worth more than one million shekels. The company "Nitsba Holdings 1995 Ltd." is engaged in managing real estate assets including offices, shopping centers, and central stations in cities such as Jerusalem, Be'er Sheva, Tel Aviv, and Netanya. The controlling owner of the group is Yoel Company, controlled by Maimon and Chaim Tzof. In the transaction in question, Airport City was merged into Nitsba and became a private company whose bonds continue to be traded. Minister Katz's fortune is estimated at tens of millions of shekels, including, allegedly, holdings in the oil and gas exploration company Isramco, which has a share in the "Tamar" gas reservoir and is controlled by Tzof, and according to various reports, also Maimon has parts of it. In 2009, the Securities Authority conducted an investigation following suspicions that Katz purchased shares of Isramco before the official report on the gas discovery and based on inside information. That investigation found nothing and the case was closed.
Summary: The text deals with suspicions against Minister Chaim Katz and his financial advisor Moti Ben Ari regarding the use of insider information. Insider information is defined as material information about a public company that has not yet been published to the general public. Using insider information for the purpose of buying or selling securities before its publication constitutes a criminal offense. According to the suspicion, Katz and Ben Ari purchased shares of the company Nitsba before the announcement of the merger deal with Airport City, and made illegal profits. Ben Ari, the financial advisor of the involved companies, is suspected of having passed the information to Katz. This is not the first time Katz has been investigated for suspected insider trading, but a previous investigation into his case was closed without filing an indictment.
Sentence in Summary: According to the suspicion, Katz and Ben Ari purchased shares of the company Nitsba before the announcement of the merger deal with Airport City, and made illegal profits
Best Match Sentences From Article: Suspected of purchasing shares of Nitsba, controlled by businessman Kobi Maimon, before a merger transaction with Airport City

Article:
Meet the youngest woman to travel the world

At just 21, Lexie Alford is the youngest person to have traveled to all 196 countries: a Guinness World Record she has been working on since childhood. Now she is writing a book and is expected to give lectures about her experiences.

On May 31, 2019, Lexie Alford, a 21-year-old American, stepped onto North Korean soil and broke a record: she is the youngest person in history to have visited every country on Earth. She broke the current Guinness record, which stood at age 24, and joined the ranks of travelers who made history, like Cassie DePecol, who at age 27 was the first woman documented to have visited every country in the world, as well as the woman who did it in the shortest amount of time.

Alford has been working on her mission to travel to all 196 independent countries in the world since childhood, even though she didn't know she was doing it at the time. Alford, a descendant of a family that owns a travel agency, says, "Traveling was part of my life before I can remember. My parents would take me out of school and let me learn independently for weeks and months each year," she recounts.

In Africa, she struggled with complicated visa issues | Screenshot from Instagram page LexieLimitless@

Growing up, Alford says she and her family traveled everywhere – from the floating villages in Cambodia to the Burj Khalifa in Dubai. "My parents placed great importance on exposing us to all forms of life in the world, and it deeply influenced the person I became," says Lexie, who has over 70,000 followers on her Instagram page, LexieLimitless@. "I have always been curious about how other people live and how they find happiness."

Breaking the Guinness Record

Alford never planned to break any records. She is just an enthusiastic traveler: "The truth is, at first, I just wanted to test the limits of my abilities, see what I could do with my life, and along the way see as much of the world as possible," she says. "Only when things started to get really challenging did I realize I was inspiring people around me, especially young women. The meaning of that support was that I couldn't give up when things got tough. I was determined to show everyone that the world is not as scary as it is portrayed in the media, and that there is kindness everywhere."

In 2016, Alford got serious and started working on her mission to break the Guinness record of James Asquith, who achieved it in 2013 at the age of 24.

Where did the idea come from? When she turned 18, Alford realized she had already visited 72 countries. "The first time I thought about breaking the world record was in October 2016, at my home in California," she says. "It was two years after I graduated high school, and I was studying at a local college. Then the idea of taking a gap year came to me, and I decided to work on breaking the record full-time."

Alford says her travels were all self-funded. She received some contracts and campaigns along the way that helped her finance the project, but she never had an official sponsor. "I always knew I would want some free time for traveling, so I worked every job I could find since I turned 12, and saved the money," she says.

The money she saved was enough for a year and a half of travel. After that, every time she returned home to Nevada City, California, she worked as a travel consultant at the family agency and did photography and blogging during her travels. "I do a lot of research in advance to find the best deals, optimally use the points and miles I've accumulated from flights, stay in cheap places like hostels, or create content for hotels in exchange for accommodation," Alford says. "I made sure my monthly expenses were as low as possible because I lived at home with my parents, I have no car expenses or student loans to pay, and I don't waste my money on unnecessary material possessions."

"I have never used a foreign SIM card"

On her Instagram, Alford gives advice on how to reconnect with the world – by disconnecting. "Some people think it's strange, but the truth is I've never used a foreign SIM card," she says. "If I need something, I start talking to the people around me. I try to stay present as much as possible when I'm exploring these places because it's a huge privilege, the fact that I have the opportunity to travel like this. I don't want to miss a single moment of that privilege."

Alford says the highlights of her travels were visits to unexpected and often dangerous countries. "Countries with a bad reputation that people are afraid to go to ignite my passion," she says. "I experienced much more kindness and natural beauty in places like Venezuela and Pakistan than in regular tourist destinations. When you arrive somewhere without expectations, you are amazed by what you can find there, and that is the most rewarding part of this project."

Marked the last country | Screenshot from Instagram page LexieLimitless@

In West and Central Africa, she really struggled due to complicated visa issues, tourism infrastructure, language barriers, and the high cost of traveling safely. "There aren't many flights, hotels, or English-speaking guides, so those who do offer these services completely dominate the market," she explains. "They can set whatever outrageous price they want because you don't really have another option. Traveling in these areas of the world has toughened me more than anything else in my life."

Another extreme challenge was marking the last country on her list: North Korea. After months and years of trying to circumvent the American travel ban, she finally got the opportunity to travel to North Korea last May, thanks to a loophole in the law that allowed her to step – just one step – onto the country's soil. According to the rules of the Guinness World Records, visiting the North Korean side of the meeting rooms in the legendary "Blue House" in the demilitarized zone is considered a visit to North Korea. "I am really very disappointed that I didn't get to visit that country properly due to political issues," she says. "But I will visit it again as soon as the ban is lifted."

Alford is currently in the process of submitting evidence to Guinness: she has over 10,000 pieces of information that she needs to organize chronologically, but her record clock stopped on the day she entered the 196th country. When she arrived in North Korea, she said she felt only one thing: "Relief. In the last six months, I dealt with immense anxiety, needing to overcome obstacles and reach some of the least accessible places in the world," she says. "Only at that moment, in that strange meeting room, did I truly realize how far I had come."

Cassie DePecol, the woman who visited all the countries in the fastest time, faced criticism at the time for not experiencing each country in depth, as she visited some for no more than two days. While it is likely that 21-year-old Alford will receive similar reactions for achieving the goal at such a young age, she is not worried. "At the end of the day, each of us prefers to travel differently," Alford says. "Some people prefer to spend months or years in a limited number of countries, while others prefer a tasting menu of the world. No matter what you prefer, there will always be someone who disagrees with you."

Alford says she took her time in most places. "I love to get away from the capitals, taste local foods, photograph nature and culture, and stay as long as I can afford it," she says. But there were countries where she felt uncomfortable traveling alone. "To be honest, my budget didn't allow me to stay weeks in every country I visited. The minimum I stayed in each place was two or three days. South Sudan, Somalia, Mali, and Chad are some of the countries where I didn't linger because I didn't have the means to fund proper security for myself. I would be happy to return and visit them in depth in the future," she says. "I feel like I only scratched the surface of everything there is to see there."

So what's next? "I am currently writing a book about my experiences in each country and the things I've learned along the way. I will also start with lectures, initially TedX. After finally accomplishing this massive mission, I will take a few months for myself to nourish my body and soul while I plan my future."

Lexie's Lessons

Alford says she doesn't know what the most important thing she learned from the whole experience is. "That's a question that will take me a long time to unpack," she says. But there are a few things she learned from her travels around the world, and these are pieces of advice anyone can use.

1. Everything is possible.
2. Don't waste your money on unnecessary material possessions – save as much as you can, move back in with your parents if you can. If traveling around the world is really important to you, you will need to make sacrifices for it.
3. There is more good than bad in the world.
4. You will never run out of beautiful places to experience.
5. You can reach every country, and we can learn so much everywhere.
6. Every time I disconnect from Wi-Fi, I can connect meaningfully to the things happening around me, which is rare in our time.
7. My advice to young people who want to do this? Believe that you can do anything you decide to do. If you make travel a priority, it's just a matter of time before you achieve it.

"I do a lot of research in advance to find the best deals, optimally use the points and miles I've accumulated from flights, stay in cheap places like hostels, or create content for hotels in exchange for accommodation," Alford says. "I made sure my monthly expenses were as low as possible because I lived at home with my parents, I have no car expenses or student loans to pay, and I don't waste my money on unnecessary material possessions."

"I have never used a foreign SIM card"

On her Instagram, Alford gives advice on how to reconnect with the world – by disconnecting. "Some people think it's strange, but the truth is I've never used a foreign SIM card," she says. "If I need something, I start talking to the people around me. I try to stay present as much as possible when I'm exploring these places because it's a huge privilege, the fact that I have the opportunity to travel like this. I don't want to miss a single moment of that privilege."

Alford says the highlights of her travels were visits to unexpected and often dangerous countries. "Countries with a bad reputation that people are afraid to go to ignite my passion," she says. "I experienced much more kindness and natural beauty in places like Venezuela and Pakistan than in regular tourist destinations. When you arrive somewhere without expectations, you are amazed by what you can find there, and that is the most rewarding part of this project."

Marked the last country | Screenshot from Instagram page LexieLimitless@

In West and Central Africa, she really struggled due to complicated visa issues, tourism infrastructure, language barriers, and the high cost of traveling safely. "There aren't many flights, hotels, or English-speaking guides, so those who do offer these services completely dominate the market," she explains. "They can set whatever outrageous price they want because you don't really have another option. Traveling in these areas of the world has toughened me more than anything else in my life."

Another extreme challenge was marking the last country on her list: North Korea. After months and years of trying to circumvent the American travel ban, she finally got the opportunity to travel to North Korea last May, thanks to a loophole in the law that allowed her to step – just one step – onto the country's soil. According to the rules of the Guinness World Records, visiting the North Korean side of the meeting rooms in the legendary "Blue House" in the demilitarized zone is considered a visit to North Korea. "I am really very disappointed that I didn't get to visit that country properly due to political issues," she says. "But I will visit it again as soon as the ban is lifted."

Alford is currently in the process of submitting evidence to Guinness: she has over 10,000 pieces of information that she needs to organize chronologically, but her record clock stopped on the day she entered the 196th country. When she arrived in North Korea, she said she felt only one thing: "Relief. In the last six months, I dealt with immense anxiety, needing to overcome obstacles and reach some of the least accessible places in the world," she says. "Only at that moment, in that strange meeting room, did I truly realize how far I had come."

Cassie DePecol, the woman who visited all the countries in the fastest time, faced criticism at the time for not experiencing each country in depth, as she visited some for no more than two days. While it is likely that 21-year-old Alford will receive similar reactions for achieving the goal at such a young age, she is not worried. "At the end of the day, each of us prefers to travel differently," Alford says. "Some people prefer to spend months or years in a limited number of countries, while others prefer a tasting menu of the world. No matter what you prefer, there will always be someone who disagrees with you."

Alford says she took her time in most places. "I love to get away from the capitals, taste local foods, photograph nature and culture, and stay as long as I can afford it," she says. But there were countries where she felt uncomfortable traveling alone. "To be honest, my budget didn't allow me to stay weeks in every country I visited. The minimum I stayed in each place was two or three days. South Sudan, Somalia, Mali, and Chad are some of the countries where I didn't linger because I didn't have the means to fund proper security for myself. I would be happy to return and visit them in depth in the future," she says. "I feel like I only scratched the surface of everything there is to see there."

So what's next? "I am currently writing a book about my experiences in each country and the things I've learned along the way. I will also start with lectures, initially TedX. After finally accomplishing this massive mission, I will take a few months for myself to nourish my body and soul while I plan my future."

Lexie's Lessons

Alford says she doesn't know what the most important thing she learned from the whole experience is. "That's a question that will take me a long time to unpack," she says. But there are a few things she learned from her travels around the world, and these are pieces of advice anyone can use.

1. Everything is possible.
2. Don't waste your money on unnecessary material possessions – save as much as you can, move back in with your parents if you can. If traveling around the world is really important to you, you will need to make sacrifices for it.
3. There is more good than bad in the world.
4. You will never run out of beautiful places to experience.
5. You can reach every country, and we can learn so much everywhere.
6. Every time I disconnect from Wi-Fi, I can connect meaningfully to the things happening around me, which is rare in our time.
7. My advice to young people who want to do this? Believe that you can do anything you decide to do. If you make travel a priority, it's just a matter of time before you achieve it.
